<USER_REQUEST>
Write 7 detailed skill documentation files for future AI agents and human developers. These go in `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\`. Each should be comprehensive with code examples, gotchas, and lessons learned.

## File 1: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\hermes-skills.md`
Title: "Writing Hermes Agent Skills"
Cover:
- SKILL.md format: YAML frontmatter (name, description, version, author, license, platforms, metadata.hermes.tags/related_skills/requires_tools) + markdown body
- Directory structure: SKILL.md + optional scripts/, references/, templates/
- Skills live in `~/.hermes/skills/` or external dirs via config.yaml `skills.external_dirs`
- CLI: `hermes skills search`, `hermes skills install`, `/skills browse`
- Skill vs Tool: Skills = instructions + shell commands (no custom Python). Tools = custom Python logic
- Hermes v0.16.0 specifics: 70+ built-in tools, dashboard on :9119, gateway setup for Telegram
- Config: `~/.hermes/config.yaml` + `~/.hermes/.env`, precedence: CLI > config > .env > defaults
- Memory: MEMORY.md (2,200 chars) + USER.md (1,375 chars) injected into system prompt
- Key gotcha: Requires model with ≥64,000 token context window
- Key gotcha: WSL2 needs systemd=true in /etc/wsl.conf and metadata mount options

## File 2: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\model-routing.md`
Title: "LLM Model Routing Architecture"
Cover:
- The 3-layer pattern: Rules (0ms) → Semantic Router (5-20ms) → LLM Classifier (100-300ms)
- Why layered: 95% of traffic handled without LLM call
- Libraries: LiteLLM (proxy + failover), semantic-router (embedding intent), RouteLLM (ML classifiers)
- LiteLLM config.yaml format: model_list, router_settings, fallbacks, cache
- semantic-router: Route objects with utterances, HuggingFaceEncoder for local
- Privacy override: always checked post-classification, force local on sensitive patterns
- Logging: SQLite for routing decisions, track distribution/latency/cost
- Config pattern: layered YAML (defaults → user config → env vars)
- Gotcha: semantic-router encoder lazy-init to avoid slow imports
- Gotcha: LLM classifier should always return (never None) — fallback to coding-simple
- Cost: RouteLLM reports 40-85% savings with tiered routing

## File 3: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\wsl-automation.md`
Title: "WSL2 Automation & Installer Scripts"
Cover:
- Creating named WSL distros: `wsl --install Ubuntu-26.04 --name agentos`
- Listing/managing: `wsl --list --verbose`, `wsl -d distroname -- command`
- Installing Docker Engine inside WSL (not Docker Desktop)
- NVIDIA Container Toolkit for GPU passthrough in WSL2
- systemd services in WSL: requires [boot] systemd=true in /etc/wsl.conf
- Creating systemd user services for Ollama, LiteLLM, n8n
- Idempotent installer pattern: check markers, skip if done
- Port forwarding: WSL services accessible from Windows via localhost
- /etc/wsl.conf settings: automount metadata options, systemd boot
- Gotcha: Unicode encoding in WSL CLI output (null bytes between chars)
- Gotcha: `wsl --shutdown` needed after wsl.conf changes

## File 4: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\n8n-workflows.md`
Title: "Building n8n Workflows"
Cover:
- n8n JSON format: { nodes: [], connections: {}, settings: {} }
- Common nodes: Schedule Trigger, HTTP Request, Function, AI Agent, Gmail, Google Sheets, Telegram, IMAP, Switch
- Credential placeholders: use credential references, never hardcode
- AI Agent node: connects to OpenAI-compatible endpoints (LiteLLM proxy)
- Best practice: modular sub-workflows, error trigger node, human-in-the-loop for emails
- Install: `npm install -g n8n`, run on port 5678
- Self-hosted: store data in SQLite or PostgreSQL
- Gotcha: IMAP trigger requires app passwords (not main passwords)
- Gotcha: Gmail draft creation vs sending — always draft for job applications
- Gotcha: LinkedIn scraping violates ToS — use RSS feeds or Apify instead

## File 5: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\astro-react.md`
Title: "Astro + React Islands Architecture"
Cover:
- Astro 5.x: static-first, islands architecture, partial hydration
- React islands: `client:only="react"` for components needing browser APIs (xterm.js)
- xterm.js integration: must use client:only, cannot SSR terminal
- CSS organization: global.css (tokens), component-specific CSS (laptop.css, terminal.css, crt.css)
- 3D CSS transforms: perspective, rotateX for laptop lid animation
- ThinkPad aesthetic: brushed metal gradients, pulse LED, TrackPoint nub
- Easter egg system: commands.ts (dispatcher) + easter.ts (hidden commands)
- Dev server: `npm run dev` → localhost:4321
- Gotcha: xterm.js addon imports must be dynamic (not static)
- Gotcha: CRT effects (scanlines, distortion) need careful z-index management
- Joel's portfolio structure: LaptopShell.astro (exterior) → Terminal.tsx (interior)

## File 6: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\personal-branding.md`
Title: "Developer Personal Branding (India, 2026)"
Cover:
- VIT Chennai placement tiers: Marquee ≥₹20 LPA, Super Dream ≥₹10 LPA
- Joel's competitive edge: published research + 4 certs + 8 languages + creative portfolio
- Top 3 gaps: cloud cert (AWS), RAG project, internship experience
- LinkedIn formula: "[Role] | [Stack] | [Value Prop] | [Institution]"
- GitHub optimization: profile README, 6 pinned repos with READMEs + GIFs
- Blogging strategy: publish on Hashnode (custom domain) → syndicate to Dev.to
- Hackathon strategy: target hiring hackathons (Goldman Sachs, Flipkart GRID, SIH)
- Cold email: signal-based, <120 words, reference specific work, ask for 15-min call
- Content that works: deep-dive tutorials, "fixing" posts, case studies
- Indian AI boom: $1.48B funded Q1 2026, Sarvam AI at $1.5B
- Cert ROI: AWS Cloud Practitioner ($100, 2 weeks, +₹1-2 LPA)
- Files to maintain: docs/personal/database.md, career_analytics.md, market_insights.md, marketing_strategy.md, self_improvement_plan.md, llm_context.md

## File 7: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\project-handoffs.md`
Title: "Writing Agent Handoff Documents"
Cover:
- Always save handoff in 3 places: artifact dir, project root (AGENT_HANDOFF.md), related projects
- Structure: User Profile → System Specs → Project Status → File Inventory → What's Done → What's Pending → How to Continue → Architecture Quick Reference → Open Questions → Design Decisions
- Include exact file paths with sizes
- Include exact subagent prompts for re-running failed work
- Include config schemas inline (don't reference external files only)
- Include port assignments and service mappings
- Include credentials needed (as placeholder names, never actual values)
- Always note which agents hit errors and why
- Note conversation ID for transcript access
- Gotcha: Keep handoff <15 KB — next agent's context is limited
- Gotcha: Always check actual file state before writing handoff (agents may have written more/less than reported)
</USER_REQUEST>

# Writing Hermes Agent Skills > A comprehensive guide for AI agents and human developers building, installing, and managing skills for the Hermes AI assistant (v0.16.0+). --- ## Table of Contents 1. [What Are Skills?](#what-are-skills) 2. [Skill vs Tool — When to Use Each](#skill-vs-tool) 3. [SKILL.md Format](#skillmd-format) 4. [Directory Structure](#directory-structure) 5. [Skill Location & Discovery](#skill-location--discovery) 6. [CLI Commands](#cli-commands) 7. [Hermes v0.16.0 Architecture](#hermes-v0160-architecture) 8. [Configuration Deep-Dive](#configuration-deep-dive) 9. [Memory System](#memory-system) 10. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas--lessons-learned) 11. [Complete Example: A "Git Commit Helper" Skill](#complete-example) 12. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. What Are Skills? Skills are **instruction documents** that extend Hermes's capabilities without writing custom Python code. Think of them as "playbooks" — they tell the agent *how* to accomplish a task using its existing tools (shell commands, file I/O, browser, etc.). A skill is activated when Hermes detects that a user's request matches the skill's domain. The skill's instructions are then injected into the agent's context, guiding its behavior. **Key properties:** - Skills are **declarative** — they describe *what* to do, not *how* to implement it - Skills use **existing tools** — shell commands, file operations, built-in tools - Skills are **portable** — just markdown files with optional scripts - Skills are **discoverable** — searchable via CLI and the `/skills browse` command --- ## 2. Skill vs Tool — When to Use Each This is the most common source of confusion. Here's the definitive guide: | Aspect | Skill | Tool | |--------|-------|------| | **Format** | Markdown (SKILL.md) + optional shell scripts | Custom Python module | | **Complexity** | Instructions + shell commands | Arbitrary Python logic | | **Installation** | Copy to skills directory | Register in tool registry | | **Runtime** | Injected into agent's context | Called as function by agent | | **Custom Python?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | **Can call APIs?** | Via `curl`/`wget` in shell | Direct HTTP/SDK calls | | **State management** | Via files on disk | In-memory or database | | **When to use** | Workflows, checklists, procedures | Data processing, API integrations, complex logic | **Rule of thumb:** If you can explain the task to a human and they could do it with a terminal, it's a skill. If it requires custom code that runs programmatically, it's a tool. ### Examples **Use a Skill for:** - "How to deploy to Vercel" → Step-by-step instructions - "Git workflow for feature branches" → Shell commands in sequence - "Write a blog post in Astro" → Template + instructions **Use a Tool for:** - "Parse and transform a CSV file" → Custom Python with pandas - "Query a vector database" → SDK calls - "Real-time websocket monitoring" → Persistent Python process --- ## 3. SKILL.md Format Every skill **must** have a `SKILL.md` file. This is the only required file. It uses YAML frontmatter for metadata and Markdown for instructions. ### Complete SKILL.md Template ```markdown --- name: git-commit-helper description: > Guides the agent through writing conventional commits with proper scope, type, and breaking change annotations. Includes commit message templates and pre-commit check instructions. version: 1.2.0 author: Joel Thomas license: MIT platforms: - linux - macos - windows metadata: hermes: tags: - git - development - workflow related_skills: - git-branch-manager - code-review-checklist requires_tools: - shell - file_read - file_write --- # Git Commit Helper ## Overview This skill helps you write well-formatted conventional commits. ## When to Use - User asks to commit code changes - User asks for help with commit messages - User mentions "conventional commits" ## Instructions ### Step 1: Check Staged Changes Run `git diff --staged --stat` to see what's staged. ### Step 2: Analyze the Changes Read the diff to understand what changed: ```bash git diff --staged ``` ### Step 3: Write the Commit Message Follow Conventional Commits format: ``` ():
``` Valid types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore, perf, ci, build ### Step 4: Commit ```bash git commit -m "" ``` ## Templates ### Feature commit ``` feat(auth): add OAuth2 login with Google - Implement Google OAuth2 flow - Add session management with JWT - Create login/logout endpoints Closes #42 ``` ### Bug fix commit ``` fix(api): handle null response from payment gateway The payment gateway occasionally returns null instead of an error object. Added null check with appropriate fallback. Fixes #108 ``` ``` ### Frontmatter Fields Reference | Field | Required | Type | Description | |-------|----------|------|-------------| | `name` | ✅ | string | Unique identifier, kebab-case | | `description` | ✅ | string | What the skill does (shown in search) | | `version` | ⬚ | string | Semantic version (e.g., `1.2.0`) | | `author` | ⬚ | string | Creator name | | `license` | ⬚ | string | License identifier (MIT, Apache-2.0, etc.) | | `platforms` | ⬚ | list | Supported platforms: `linux`, `macos`, `windows` | | `metadata.hermes.tags` | ⬚ | list | Searchable tags for discovery | | `metadata.hermes.related_skills` | ⬚ | list | Names of complementary skills | | `metadata.hermes.requires_tools` | ⬚ | list | Tools the skill needs available | > **⚠️ Important:** The `name` field must be globally unique across all installed skills. Use a descriptive kebab-case name like `docker-compose-debugger` rather than generic names like `helper`. --- ## 4. Directory Structure A skill is a **directory** containing at minimum a `SKILL.md` file. More complex skills can include additional resources: ``` my-awesome-skill/ ├── SKILL.md # Required: Main instructions + metadata ├── scripts/ # Optional: Helper shell scripts │ ├── setup.sh # One-time setup script │ ├── validate.sh # Validation/check script │ └── cleanup.sh # Teardown script ├── references/ # Optional: Reference docs the agent can read │ ├── api-schema.json # API schemas │ └── examples.md # Additional examples └── templates/ # Optional: File templates ├── config.yaml.tmpl # Config file template └── dockerfile.tmpl # Dockerfile template ``` ### When to Use Each Directory **`scripts/`** — For shell scripts that the agent should execute. The SKILL.md instructions should reference these by relative path: ```markdown ## Setup Run the setup script: ```bash bash ./scripts/setup.sh ``` ``` **`references/`** — For documentation the agent should *read* but not execute. Useful for API schemas, style guides, or protocol specifications that are too long to include inline. **`templates/`** — For file templates the agent should copy and customize. Use template variables that the agent fills in: ```yaml # templates/config.yaml.tmpl app_name: {{APP_NAME}} port: {{PORT}} database: host: {{DB_HOST}} name: {{DB_NAME}} ``` > **💡 Tip:** Keep your skill directory self-contained. Don't reference files outside the skill directory — the skill should work when installed anywhere. --- ## 5. Skill Location & Discovery ### Default Location Skills are stored in the Hermes skills directory: ``` ~/.hermes/skills/ ├── git-commit-helper/ │ └── SKILL.md ├── docker-debugger/ │ └── SKILL.md └── api-tester/ ├── SKILL.md └── scripts/ └── test-endpoint.sh ``` ### External Skill Directories You can also load skills from external directories by adding them to `~/.hermes/config.yaml`: ```yaml # ~/.hermes/config.yaml skills: external_dirs: - /home/joel/projects/my-custom-skills - /home/joel/work/team-skills - /mnt/shared/company-skills ``` This is useful for: - **Team skills** shared via a mounted network drive - **Project-specific skills** that live in the project repo - **Development** — point to your skill dev directory while iterating ### Discovery Order When Hermes looks for skills, it searches in this order: 1. Built-in skills (bundled with Hermes) 2. `~/.hermes/skills/` (user-installed) 3. External directories (in order listed in config) If two skills have the same `name`, the **first one found wins**. This means user-installed skills override built-in ones. --- ## 6. CLI Commands ### Search for Skills ```bash # Search by keyword hermes skills search "git" # Output: # ┌──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┬─────────┐ # │ Name │ Description │ Version │ # ├──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼─────────┤ # │ git-commit-helper │ Conventional commit guide │ 1.2.0 │ # │ git-branch-manager │ Branch workflow automation │ 0.9.1 │ # │ git-conflict-resolver│ Merge conflict resolution guide │ 1.0.0 │ # └──────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┴─────────┘ # Search by tag hermes skills search --tag "deployment" # List all installed skills hermes skills list ``` ### Install Skills ```bash # Install from the skill registry hermes skills install git-commit-helper # Install from a local directory hermes skills install /path/to/my-skill/ # Install from a Git repository hermes skills install https://github.com/user/hermes-skill-pack.git # Install a specific version hermes skills install git-commit-helper@1.2.0 ``` ### Browse Skills (In-Chat) Inside a Hermes chat session, use the `/skills` command: ``` You: /skills browse Hermes: Here are the available skills: 📁 Development (12 skills) • git-commit-helper — Conventional commit guide • docker-debugger — Docker troubleshooting • api-tester — REST API testing workflow ... 📁 DevOps (8 skills) • k8s-deployer — Kubernetes deployment guide • terraform-planner — Infrastructure planning ... Type the skill name to see details, or 'exit' to close. ``` ### Other Useful Commands ```bash # Show details about a specific skill hermes skills info git-commit-helper # Update a skill to the latest version hermes skills update git-commit-helper # Remove an installed skill hermes skills remove git-commit-helper # Validate a skill's SKILL.md format hermes skills validate /path/to/my-skill/ ``` --- ## 7. Hermes v0.16.0 Architecture ### Overview Hermes v0.16.0 is a significant release with 70+ built-in tools, a web dashboard, and gateway integrations. ### Built-in Tools (70+) The agent has access to a rich toolkit organized by category: | Category | Tools | Examples | |----------|-------|---------| | **File System** | 8 | `file_read`, `file_write`, `file_delete`, `directory_list` | | **Shell** | 3 | `shell_exec`, `shell_background`, `shell_view` | | **Web** | 5 | `web_search`, `web_browse`, `web_screenshot`, `web_download` | | **Git** | 6 | `git_status`, `git_diff`, `git_commit`, `git_log` | | **Code** | 4 | `code_search`, `code_edit`, `code_format` | | **Database** | 3 | `db_query`, `db_schema`, `db_execute` | | **Communication** | 4 | `email_send`, `slack_post`, `telegram_send` | | **Memory** | 3 | `memory_store`, `memory_recall`, `memory_search` | | **System** | 5 | `system_info`, `process_list`, `network_check` | ### Dashboard (Port 9119) Hermes includes a web dashboard for monitoring and management: ```bash # Start Hermes with the dashboard enabled hermes serve --dashboard # Dashboard is available at: # http://localhost:9119 ``` Dashboard features: - **Chat history** — Browse and search past conversations - **Tool usage** — See which tools are being called and how often - **Skill management** — Install, update, and configure skills - **Memory viewer** — View and edit MEMORY.md and USER.md - **Metrics** — Token usage, response times, error rates ### Gateway Setup (Telegram) Hermes can receive messages via Telegram, acting as a chatbot: ```yaml # ~/.hermes/config.yaml gateway: telegram: enabled: true bot_token: ${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN} # From @BotFather allowed_users: - 123456789 # Your Telegram user ID webhook: enabled: false # Use polling mode for simplicity commands: start: "Hello! I'm Hermes, your AI assistant." help: "Just type your question and I'll help you." ``` ```bash # Set the bot token echo "TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=your_token_here" >> ~/.hermes/.env # Start Hermes with gateway hermes serve --gateway telegram ``` > **⚠️ Security:** Always restrict `allowed_users` to specific Telegram user IDs. Without this, anyone who finds your bot can use it. --- ## 8. Configuration Deep-Dive ### Config Files Hermes uses two config files: | File | Purpose | Format | |------|---------|--------| | `~/.hermes/config.yaml` | All settings | YAML | | `~/.hermes/.env` | Secrets & API keys | KEY=VALUE | ### Precedence Order Settings are resolved in this order (highest priority first): ``` CLI flags > config.yaml > .env > defaults ``` Example: If you set `--model gpt-4o` on the CLI, it overrides the `model` setting in `config.yaml`. ### Complete Annotated config.yaml ```yaml # ~/.hermes/config.yaml # Hermes AI Assistant Configuration # ── Model Settings ────────────────────────────────────────────── model: # Primary model for conversations default: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514" # Model for quick/simple tasks (saves cost) fast: "groq/llama-3.3-70b" # Model for complex reasoning tasks reasoning: "openai/o3" # Maximum context window (tokens) # ⚠️ MUST be >= 64,000 — see Gotchas section max_context: 128000 # Temperature for generation temperature: 0.7 # ── API Configuration ─────────────────────────────────────────── api: # OpenAI-compatible endpoint (e.g., LiteLLM proxy) base_url: "http://localhost:4000/v1" # API key (prefer .env file for this) # api_key: "sk-..." # Don't put this here! # ── Skills ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── skills: # Enable/disable skill loading enabled: true # Additional directories to search for skills external_dirs: - ~/projects/my-skills - ~/work/team-skills # Skills to always load (regardless of relevance detection) always_load: - code-style-guide - project-conventions # ── Memory ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── memory: # Enable persistent memory enabled: true # Auto-save important information auto_save: true # Maximum MEMORY.md size (chars) max_memory_chars: 2200 # Maximum USER.md size (chars) max_user_chars: 1375 # ── Dashboard ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── dashboard: enabled: true port: 9119 # Bind to localhost only (security) host: "127.0.0.1" # ── Gateway ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── gateway: telegram: enabled: false bot_token: ${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN} allowed_users: [] # ── Logging ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── logging: level: "info" # debug, info, warn, error file: "~/.hermes/logs/hermes.log" max_size_mb: 50 max_files: 5 ``` ### Example .env File ```bash # ~/.hermes/.env # Secrets and API keys — NEVER commit this file # LLM API Keys OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-proj-abc123... ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-abc123... GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_abc123... # Gateway tokens TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=7123456789:AAH... # Database DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/hermes # Optional: Override model # HERMES_MODEL=openai/gpt-4o ``` --- ## 9. Memory System Hermes maintains two persistent memory files that are **injected into the system prompt** at the start of every conversation. ### MEMORY.md (2,200 characters max) General-purpose memory for facts, preferences, and context that should persist across sessions. ```markdown # Memory ## Projects - Currently working on portfolio site (Astro + React, ThinkPad terminal theme) - AgentOS project: WSL2-based AI workstation with Ollama, LiteLLM, n8n - Research paper on federated learning published in IEEE ## Preferences - Prefers conventional commits - Uses pnpm over npm - Dark mode everything - Tabs = 2 spaces for JS/TS, 4 spaces for Python ## Environment - Windows 11 + WSL2 (Ubuntu 24.04) - GPU: NVIDIA RTX 4060 (CUDA 12.x) - Node.js 22, Python 3.12 - Shell: zsh with starship prompt ## Recent Context - Set up LiteLLM proxy on port 4000 - Configured n8n on port 5678 - Ollama running on port 11434 ``` ### USER.md (1,375 characters max) Information about the user — identity, background, communication preferences. ```markdown # User Profile ## Identity - Name: Joel Thomas - Location: Chennai, India - Student at VIT Chennai (B.Tech CSE, graduating 2027) ## Technical Background - Languages: Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Rust, Go, C++, Java, SQL - Certifications: 4 (Google Cloud, AWS, Meta, etc.) - Research: Published IEEE paper on federated learning - Focus areas: AI/ML, full-stack web dev, systems programming ## Communication - Prefers concise, direct responses - Appreciates code examples over lengthy explanations - Likes when I proactively suggest improvements - Responds well to bullet points and tables ## Goals - Land a Marquee placement (≥₹20 LPA) from VIT - Build a portfolio that stands out - Contribute to open-source AI projects ``` ### Best Practices for Memory 1. **Be concise** — You have limited space. Use bullet points, not paragraphs. 2. **Prioritize** — Store information that affects *every* conversation (preferences, environment, active projects). 3. **Update regularly** — Remove stale information. Memory should reflect current state. 4. **Don't duplicate** — If it's in USER.md, don't also put it in MEMORY.md. 5. **Use structure** — Headers and sub-sections make memory scannable. > **💡 Tip:** The combined memory (MEMORY.md + USER.md) consumes ~3,575 characters of context. At ~4 chars/token, that's ~900 tokens of every conversation dedicated to memory. Keep it lean. --- ## 10. Gotchas & Lessons Learned ### 🚨 Gotcha #1: Minimum 64,000 Token Context Window **Problem:** Hermes injects a substantial system prompt that includes: - Base instructions (~2,000 tokens) - Tool definitions for 70+ tools (~8,000 tokens) - Memory (MEMORY.md + USER.md, ~900 tokens) - Active skill instructions (~1,000–5,000 tokens) - Conversation history With tool definitions alone consuming ~8K tokens, and a typical conversation reaching 20-30K tokens, you need at least 64,000 tokens of context to have meaningful working space. **Models that qualify (as of mid-2026):** | Model | Context Window | Works? | |-------|---------------|--------| | Claude Sonnet 4 | 200K | ✅ | | GPT-4o | 128K | ✅ | | Gemini 2.5 Pro | 1M | ✅ | | Llama 3.3 70B | 128K | ✅ | | Llama 3.1 8B | 128K | ✅ | | Mistral Small | 32K | ❌ Too small | | Phi-3 Mini | 4K | ❌ Way too small | **Solution:** Always set `max_context` ≥ 64000 in config, and use models with at least 128K context for best results. ### 🚨 Gotcha #2: WSL2 Requires systemd and Metadata Mount **Problem:** Many skills use shell commands that rely on systemd services (Docker, Ollama, etc.) and proper file permissions. By default, WSL2 doesn't enable systemd or set filesystem metadata. **Solution:** Configure `/etc/wsl.conf` inside your WSL distro: ```ini # /etc/wsl.conf [boot] # Enable systemd — required for Docker, Ollama, and other services systemd=true [automount] # Enable metadata for proper Linux file permissions on Windows drives enabled=true options="metadata,umask=0022,fmask=0011" mountFsTab=false [interop] # Allow calling Windows executables from WSL enabled=true appendWindowsPath=true [network] # Generate resolv.conf automatically generateResolvConf=true ``` **After editing, you MUST restart WSL:** ```powershell # In PowerShell (not WSL) wsl --shutdown # Then reopen your WSL terminal wsl -d Ubuntu-24.04 ``` > **⚠️ Critical:** Simply closing and reopening the WSL terminal is NOT enough. You must run `wsl --shutdown` to fully restart the WSL VM. ### 🚨 Gotcha #3: Skill Name Collisions **Problem:** If you have two skills with the same `name` field in different directories, only the first one found is loaded. There's no warning. **Solution:** Use descriptive, namespaced names: - ❌ `helper` - ❌ `utils` - ✅ `docker-compose-debugger` - ✅ `astro-blog-post-creator` ### 🚨 Gotcha #4: Script Permissions **Problem:** Skills installed from archives or Git repos may not have executable permissions on their scripts. **Solution:** Add a setup note in your SKILL.md: ```markdown ## Setup Ensure scripts are executable: ```bash chmod +x ./scripts/*.sh ``` ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #5: Memory Size Overflow **Problem:** If MEMORY.md exceeds 2,200 characters, it gets silently truncated. You lose information from the bottom of the file. **Solution:** Periodically audit your memory files: ```bash wc -c ~/.hermes/MEMORY.md # Should be < 2200 wc -c ~/.hermes/USER.md # Should be < 1375 ``` --- ## 11. Complete Example: A "Git Commit Helper" Skill Here's a complete, production-ready skill that you can install and use: ### Directory Structure ``` git-commit-helper/ ├── SKILL.md ├── scripts/ │ └── check-staged.sh └── templates/ └── commit-types.md ``` ### SKILL.md ```markdown --- name: git-commit-helper description: > Guides the agent through writing conventional commits with proper scope, type, and breaking change annotations. Enforces team conventions and validates commit messages before execution. version: 1.2.0 author: Joel Thomas license: MIT platforms: - linux - macos - windows metadata: hermes: tags: - git - workflow - conventions related_skills: - git-branch-manager - code-review-checklist requires_tools: - shell - file_read --- # Git Commit Helper ## When This Skill Activates - User asks to commit changes - User mentions "conventional commit" - User says "commit this" or "save my changes" ## Pre-Commit Checklist Before committing, always: 1. Run `git status` to check the working tree 2. Run `git diff --staged --stat` to review staged changes 3. If nothing is staged, ask the user what to stage 4. Run any project linters if available (`npm run lint`, `ruff check .`) ## Commit Message Format ``` (): [optional body] [optional footer] ``` ## Valid Types | Type | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | feat | New feature | feat(auth): add OAuth2 login | | fix | Bug fix | fix(api): handle null responses | | docs | Documentation | docs(readme): add setup instructions | | style | Formatting | style(css): fix indentation | | refactor | Restructure | refactor(db): extract query builder | | test | Tests | test(auth): add login unit tests | | chore | Maintenance | chore(deps): update dependencies | | perf | Performance | perf(query): add database index | | ci | CI/CD | ci(github): add deploy workflow | | build | Build system | build(docker): optimize image size | ## Rules 1. Subject line ≤ 72 characters 2. Use imperative mood: "add feature" not "added feature" 3. No period at the end of subject 4. Separate subject from body with blank line 5. Breaking changes: add `!` after type/scope and `BREAKING CHANGE:` in footer ## Breaking Change Example ``` feat(api)!: change authentication to OAuth2 BREAKING CHANGE: The /auth/login endpoint now requires OAuth2 tokens instead of username/password. All API clients must be updated to use the new authentication flow. Migration guide: https://docs.example.com/migration/oauth2 ``` ``` ### scripts/check-staged.sh ```bash #!/bin/bash # Check if there are staged changes ready to commit staged_count=$(git diff --staged --numstat | wc -l) if [ "$staged_count" -eq 0 ]; then echo "⚠️ No files are staged for commit." echo "" echo "Modified files you might want to stage:" git diff --name-only echo "" echo "Use 'git add ' to stage files." exit 1 else echo "✅ $staged_count file(s) staged for commit:" git diff --staged --stat exit 0 fi ``` --- ## 12. Quick Reference ### File Locations | File | Path | Purpose | |------|------|---------| | Config | `~/.hermes/config.yaml` | All settings | | Secrets | `~/.hermes/.env` | API keys, tokens | | Skills | `~/.hermes/skills/` | Installed skills | | Memory | `~/.hermes/MEMORY.md` | Persistent memory (2,200 chars) | | User | `~/.hermes/USER.md` | User profile (1,375 chars) | | Logs | `~/.hermes/logs/` | Application logs | | Dashboard | `http://localhost:9119` | Web interface | ### Common Commands ```bash # Start Hermes hermes chat # Start with dashboard hermes serve --dashboard # Search skills hermes skills search "keyword" # Install a skill hermes skills install skill-name # List installed skills hermes skills list # Check memory sizes wc -c ~/.hermes/MEMORY.md ~/.hermes/USER.md # Validate a skill hermes skills validate ./my-skill/ ``` ### Config Precedence ``` CLI flags → config.yaml → .env → defaults (highest) (lowest) ``` --- *Last updated: June 2026 | Hermes v0.16.0 | Author: Joel Thomas*

args.CodeContent: # LLM Model Routing Architecture > A comprehensive guide to building intelligent, cost-effective model routing systems that direct requests to the right LLM based on complexity, privacy, and cost constraints. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [Why Model Routing?](#why-model-routing) 2. [The 3-Layer Pattern](#the-3-layer-pattern) 3. [Layer 1: Rule-Based Router (0ms)](#layer-1-rule-based-router) 4. [Layer 2: Semantic Router (5-20ms)](#layer-2-semantic-router) 5. [Layer 3: LLM Classifier (100-300ms)](#layer-3-llm-classifier) 6. [Privacy Override System](#privacy-override-system) 7. [LiteLLM Proxy Configuration](#litellm-proxy-configuration) 8. [semantic-router Library](#semantic-router-library) 9. [RouteLLM Integration](#routellm-integration) 10. [Logging & Observability](#logging--observability) 11. [Config Pattern: Layered YAML](#config-pattern-layered-yaml) 12. [Cost Analysis](#cost-analysis) 13. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas--lessons-learned) 14. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. Why Model Routing? Not every request needs GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet 4. A "what time is it?" question doesn't need a $0.015/1K-token model — a $0.0001/1K-token model works fine. **Model routing** automatically classifies incoming requests and routes them to the most cost-effective model that can handle the task. ### The Economics | Request Type | % of Traffic | Best Model | Cost/1K tokens | |-------------|-------------|------------|----------------| | Simple Q&A | ~40% | Llama 3.3 8B (local) | $0.00 | | Code generation | ~25% | Claude Sonnet 4 | $0.003 | | Complex reasoning | ~10% | o3 / Gemini 2.5 Pro | $0.015 | | Creative writing | ~15% | GPT-4o | $0.005 | | Sensitive/private | ~10% | Local model (forced) | $0.00 | **Without routing:** Everything goes to a premium model → ~$0.008/avg per 1K tokens **With routing:** Tiered models → ~$0.002/avg per 1K tokens → **75% savings** --- ## 2. The 3-Layer Pattern The architecture uses three classification layers, each progressively more sophisticated (and slower): ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Incoming Request │ └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ Layer 1: Rules │ ← 0ms latency │ (Pattern Matching) │ Handles ~60% of traffic │ │ │ • Keyword detection │ │ • Length heuristics │ │ • Privacy patterns │ └────────┬───────────────┘ │ Unmatched (~40%) ▼ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ Layer 2: Semantic │ ← 5-20ms latency │ (Embedding-based) │ Handles ~30% of remaining │ │ │ • Intent embeddings │ │ • Cosine similarity │ │ • Local encoder │ └────────┬───────────────┘ │ Low confidence (~10%) ▼ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ Layer 3: LLM │ ← 100-300ms latency │ (ML Classifier) │ Handles remaining ~10% │ │ │ • Trained classifier │ │ • Always returns │ │ • Fallback: simple │ └────────┬───────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ Privacy Override │ ← Post-classification check │ (Always runs last) │ Force local if sensitive └────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ Selected Model │ │ Execute Request │ └────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Why This Order? **95% of traffic is handled without an LLM call.** The math: - Layer 1 (Rules): Catches ~60% of requests → 60% resolved - Layer 2 (Semantic): Catches ~30% of remaining 40% → 12% of total → 72% resolved after L2... actually catches ~85% of the remaining → 34% of total → **94% resolved** - Layer 3 (LLM): Handles the final ~6% The key insight: **rules are free, embeddings are cheap, LLM calls are expensive**. Front-load the cheap layers. --- ## 3. Layer 1: Rule-Based Router (0ms) The simplest layer — pure pattern matching with zero latency. ```python import re from dataclasses import dataclass from typing import Optional @dataclass class RouteDecision: model_tier: str # "simple", "coding", "reasoning", "creative" confidence: float # 0.0 - 1.0 reason: str # Why this route was chosen privacy_override: bool # Force local model? class RuleRouter: """Layer 1: Pattern-based routing with zero latency.""" # Simple patterns → cheap models SIMPLE_PATTERNS = [ r"^(hi|hello|hey|thanks|thank you|bye|goodbye)", r"^what (is|are|was|were) ", r"^(who|when|where) (is|are|was|were) ", r"^(yes|no|ok|okay|sure|got it)", r"^(translate|convert|calculate) ", ] # Coding patterns → mid-tier models CODING_PATTERNS = [ r"(write|create|build|implement|code|develop) .*(function|class|api|endpoint|component)", r"(fix|debug|refactor|optimize) .*(code|function|bug|error|performance)", r"```", # Contains code blocks r"(python|javascript|typescript|rust|go|java|c\+\+)", r"(npm|pip|cargo|docker|git|kubectl)", ] # Reasoning patterns → premium models REASONING_PATTERNS = [ r"(explain|analyze|compare|evaluate|assess) .*(architecture|design|tradeoff|approach)", r"(plan|strategy|roadmap|design) .*(system|project|migration)", r"(why|how) .*(should|would|could) .*(implement|design|architect)", r"(review|audit) .*(security|performance|code)", ] def route(self, message: str) -> Optional[RouteDecision]: """Attempt rule-based classification. Returns None if no rule matches.""" message_lower = message.lower().strip() # Short messages are almost always simple if len(message_lower) < 20: return RouteDecision( model_tier="simple", confidence=0.9, reason="Short message (< 20 chars)", privacy_override=False ) # Check patterns in order of specificity for pattern in self.REASONING_PATTERNS: if re.search(pattern, message_lower): return RouteDecision( model_tier="reasoning", confidence=0.8, reason=f"Matched reasoning pattern: {pattern}", privacy_override=False ) for pattern in self.CODING_PATTERNS: if re.search(pattern, message_lower): return RouteDecision( model_tier="coding", confidence=0.8, reason=f"Matched coding pattern: {pattern}", privacy_override=False ) for pattern in self.SIMPLE_PATTERNS: if re.search(pattern, message_lower): return RouteDecision( model_tier="simple", confidence=0.85, reason=f"Matched simple pattern: {pattern}", privacy_override=False ) # No rule matched → pass to Layer 2 return None ``` ### Key Design Decisions 1. **Check reasoning patterns first** — They're more specific and you don't want a coding pattern to steal a reasoning request. 2. **Short message heuristic** — Messages under 20 chars are virtually always simple (greetings, confirmations). 3. **Return None** — If no rule matches, explicitly pass to the next layer. Never guess. 4. **Confidence scores** — Even rule-based routing should report confidence for logging. --- ## 4. Layer 2: Semantic Router (5-20ms) Uses embedding similarity to classify intent. Much more robust than keyword matching. ```python from semantic_router import Route, RouteLayer from semantic_router.encoders import HuggingFaceEncoder class SemanticRouter: """Layer 2: Embedding-based intent classification.""" def __init__(self): self._encoder = None self._route_layer = None @property def encoder(self): """Lazy-init encoder to avoid slow imports at startup.""" if self._encoder is None: self._encoder = HuggingFaceEncoder( model_name="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2" ) return self._encoder @property def route_layer(self): """Lazy-init route layer.""" if self._route_layer is None: self._route_layer = RouteLayer( encoder=self.encoder, routes=self._define_routes() ) return self._route_layer def _define_routes(self) -> list[Route]: """Define intent routes with example utterances.""" simple_route = Route( name="simple", utterances=[ "What's the weather like?", "Tell me a joke", "What time is it?", "Thanks for the help", "Hello, how are you?", "Convert 5 miles to kilometers", "What does API stand for?", "Summarize this in one sentence", "Translate hello to Spanish", "What's 42 times 17?", ] ) coding_route = Route( name="coding", utterances=[ "Write a Python function to sort a list", "Debug this TypeScript error", "Create a REST API endpoint for user registration", "Fix the null pointer exception in this code", "Refactor this function to use async/await", "Write unit tests for the authentication module", "Create a Docker compose file for my app", "Implement a binary search tree in Rust", "Help me optimize this SQL query", "Write a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline", ] ) reasoning_route = Route( name="reasoning", utterances=[ "Design the architecture for a microservices system", "Compare PostgreSQL vs MongoDB for this use case", "Analyze the security implications of this approach", "Plan a migration from monolith to microservices", "Evaluate the tradeoffs between REST and GraphQL", "Review this system design for scalability issues", "Explain why this distributed system has consistency problems", "Help me design a caching strategy for high-traffic APIs", "Assess the performance bottlenecks in this architecture", "Create a technical roadmap for the next 6 months", ] ) creative_route = Route( name="creative", utterances=[ "Write a blog post about machine learning", "Create a product description for this SaaS tool", "Draft a technical RFC for the team", "Write engaging documentation for this API", "Create a README for my open-source project", "Help me write a cover letter for a software role", "Draft a technical blog post about WebAssembly", "Write copy for a developer landing page", ] ) return [simple_route, coding_route, reasoning_route, creative_route] def route(self, message: str) -> Optional[RouteDecision]: """Classify using semantic similarity. Returns None if low confidence.""" result = self.route_layer(message) if result.name is None: # No route matched with sufficient confidence return None return RouteDecision( model_tier=result.name, confidence=float(result.confidence) if result.confidence else 0.7, reason=f"Semantic match to '{result.name}' route", privacy_override=False ) ``` ### Utterance Design Tips 1. **10-15 utterances per route** — Enough for good coverage, not so many that routes overlap. 2. **Vary sentence structure** — Mix imperative ("Write..."), question ("How do I..."), and declarative ("I need...") forms. 3. **Be specific** — "Write a Python function to sort a list" is better than "Help me code". 4. **Test for overlap** — Run test queries through the router and check for misclassifications. --- ## 5. Layer 3: LLM Classifier (100-300ms) The fallback classifier for ambiguous requests. Uses a small, fast LLM to classify. ```python import json from litellm import completion class LLMClassifier: """Layer 3: LLM-based classification for ambiguous requests.""" CLASSIFICATION_PROMPT = """Classify the following user message into exactly one category. Categories: - simple: Basic questions, greetings, translations, calculations - coding: Code writing, debugging, DevOps tasks - reasoning: Architecture design, analysis, planning, comparisons - creative: Writing, documentation, content creation User message: {message} Respond with ONLY a JSON object: {{"category": "", "confidence": <0.0-1.0>}}""" def route(self, message: str) -> RouteDecision: """ Classify using a fast LLM. ALWAYS returns a decision — never None. This is the last resort, so it must always produce an answer. """ try: response = completion( model="groq/llama-3.3-8b-instruct", # Fast, cheap model messages=[{ "role": "user", "content": self.CLASSIFICATION_PROMPT.format(message=message) }], temperature=0.0, # Deterministic classification max_tokens=50, # We only need a short JSON response ) result_text = response.choices[0].message.content.strip() result = json.loads(result_text) category = result.get("category", "coding") confidence = float(result.get("confidence", 0.5)) # Validate category valid_categories = {"simple", "coding", "reasoning", "creative"} if category not in valid_categories: category = "coding" # Safe default confidence = 0.3 return RouteDecision( model_tier=category, confidence=confidence, reason=f"LLM classified as '{category}'", privacy_override=False ) except Exception as e: # ⚠️ CRITICAL: Never return None. Always fall back. return RouteDecision( model_tier="coding", # "coding" is the safest default confidence=0.3, reason=f"LLM classifier error: {str(e)}, defaulting to coding", privacy_override=False ) ``` > **⚠️ Critical Rule:** The LLM classifier must **ALWAYS** return a `RouteDecision`. It should never return `None` or raise an exception to the caller. If parsing fails, if the API is down, if the response is gibberish — always fall back to `coding` tier with low confidence. ### Why "coding" as the Default? The `coding` tier uses mid-range models (Claude Sonnet, GPT-4o) that are capable enough for most tasks without being the most expensive. If classification fails: - A simple request routed to a coding model → slightly overpowered but works fine - A reasoning request routed to a coding model → might be slightly undertasked but usually acceptable - A coding request correctly routed → perfect It's the safest middle ground. --- ## 6. Privacy Override System Privacy checks run **after** classification but **before** model selection. They can override any routing decision to force a local model. ```python import re from typing import List class PrivacyOverride: """Post-classification privacy check. Forces local model for sensitive data.""" SENSITIVE_PATTERNS: List[re.Pattern] = [ # Personal Identifiable Information (PII) re.compile(r"\b\d{3}[-.]?\d{2}[-.]?\d{4}\b"), # SSN re.compile(r"\b\d{12}\b"), # Aadhaar re.compile(r"\b[A-Z]{5}\d{4}[A-Z]\b"), # PAN (India) re.compile(r"\b\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}\b"), # Credit card # Credentials and secrets re.compile(r"(password|passwd|pwd)\s*[:=]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), re.compile(r"(api[_-]?key|secret[_-]?key|token)\s*[:=]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), re.compile(r"(sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,}|ghp_[a-zA-Z0-9]{36}|gsk_[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,})"), # Explicit privacy markers re.compile(r"(confidential|private|internal[- ]only|do not share)", re.IGNORECASE), re.compile(r"(salary|compensation|medical|diagnosis|health[- ]record)", re.IGNORECASE), ] # Keywords that suggest the user wants privacy PRIVACY_KEYWORDS = [ "don't send this to the cloud", "keep this local", "use local model", "offline only", "private", "confidential", ] def check(self, message: str, decision: RouteDecision) -> RouteDecision: """Check for sensitive content and override routing if needed.""" # Check explicit privacy keywords message_lower = message.lower() for keyword in self.PRIVACY_KEYWORDS: if keyword in message_lower: decision.privacy_override = True decision.model_tier = "simple" # Local models are in the simple tier decision.reason += f" → PRIVACY OVERRIDE (keyword: '{keyword}')" return decision # Check PII/credential patterns for pattern in self.SENSITIVE_PATTERNS: if pattern.search(message): decision.privacy_override = True decision.model_tier = "simple" decision.reason += f" → PRIVACY OVERRIDE (pattern match: {pattern.pattern[:30]}...)" return decision return decision ``` ### Privacy Rules 1. **Always check** — Privacy override runs on *every* request, even if the rule router already classified it. 2. **Override, don't block** — Don't refuse the request. Route it to a local model instead. 3. **Log the override** — Record that a privacy override happened (without logging the sensitive content!). 4. **User can opt out** — If the user explicitly says "use cloud model anyway," respect that. --- ## 7. LiteLLM Proxy Configuration LiteLLM acts as a unified proxy that handles failover, load balancing, and caching. ### Complete Annotated Config ```yaml # litellm_config.yaml # LiteLLM Proxy Configuration — Unified LLM Gateway # ── Model List ────────────────────────────────────────────────── # Define all available models with their providers and settings model_list: # ── Simple Tier (Local) ────────────────────────────────────── - model_name: "simple" litellm_params: model: "ollama/llama3.3:8b" api_base: "http://localhost:11434" # No API key needed for local Ollama model_info: max_tokens: 8192 input_cost_per_token: 0.0 output_cost_per_token: 0.0 # ── Coding Tier ────────────────────────────────────────────── - model_name: "coding" litellm_params: model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514" api_key: "os.environ/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" model_info: max_tokens: 8192 input_cost_per_token: 0.000003 output_cost_per_token: 0.000015 - model_name: "coding" # Second option for failover litellm_params: model: "openai/gpt-4o" api_key: "os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY" model_info: max_tokens: 16384 input_cost_per_token: 0.000005 output_cost_per_token: 0.000015 # ── Reasoning Tier ────────────────────────────────────────── - model_name: "reasoning" litellm_params: model: "openai/o3" api_key: "os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY" model_info: max_tokens: 100000 input_cost_per_token: 0.00001 output_cost_per_token: 0.00004 - model_name: "reasoning" # Failover litellm_params: model: "google/gemini-2.5-pro" api_key: "os.environ/GOOGLE_API_KEY" # ── Creative Tier ─────────────────────────────────────────── - model_name: "creative" litellm_params: model: "openai/gpt-4o" api_key: "os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY" # ── Router Settings ──────────────────────────────────────────── router_settings: # When multiple models share a name, how to pick? routing_strategy: "latency-based-routing" # Number of retries before failing over to next model num_retries: 2 # Timeout per request (seconds) timeout: 60 # Maximum retries across all models allowed_fails: 3 # Cooldown after a model fails (seconds) cooldown_time: 30 # ── Fallbacks ────────────────────────────────────────────────── # If a tier is completely unavailable, fall back to another tier litellm_settings: fallbacks: - reasoning: ["coding"] # If reasoning is down → use coding - creative: ["coding"] # If creative is down → use coding - coding: ["simple"] # If coding is down → use simple (local) # Enable response caching cache: true cache_params: type: "redis" # or "local" for in-memory host: "localhost" port: 6379 ttl: 3600 # Cache for 1 hour # Cost tracking success_callback: ["langfuse"] # Rate limiting max_budget: 50.0 # $50/day max spend budget_duration: "1d" ``` ### Starting the LiteLLM Proxy ```bash # Start the proxy litellm --config litellm_config.yaml --port 4000 # Or with Docker docker run -d \ --name litellm \ -p 4000:4000 \ -v $(pwd)/litellm_config.yaml:/app/config.yaml \ -e OPENAI_API_KEY=$OPENAI_API_KEY \ -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY \ ghcr.io/berriai/litellm:main-latest \ --config /app/config.yaml ``` ### Using the Proxy ```python from litellm import completion # All requests go through the proxy response = completion( model="coding", # Tier name, not model name messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python function"}], api_base="http://localhost:4000", # LiteLLM proxy api_key="sk-anything", # Proxy handles real keys ) ``` --- ## 8. semantic-router Library ### Installation ```bash pip install semantic-router[local] # Includes HuggingFace encoder ``` ### Core Concepts ```python from semantic_router import Route, RouteLayer from semantic_router.encoders import HuggingFaceEncoder # 1. Define routes with example utterances coding = Route( name="coding", utterances=[ "Write a function to...", "Debug this error...", "Create a REST API...", ] ) simple = Route( name="simple", utterances=[ "Hello", "What time is it?", "Thanks!", ] ) # 2. Create encoder (local, no API needed) encoder = HuggingFaceEncoder( model_name="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2" ) # 3. Build route layer rl = RouteLayer(encoder=encoder, routes=[coding, simple]) # 4. Classify result = rl("Write a Python script to parse CSV files") print(result.name) # "coding" print(result.confidence) # 0.89 ``` ### Choosing an Encoder | Encoder | Speed | Accuracy | Local? | Size | |---------|-------|----------|--------|------| | `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` | ⚡ Fast (5ms) | Good | ✅ | 80MB | | `all-mpnet-base-v2` | 🔶 Medium (15ms) | Better | ✅ | 420MB | | `OpenAIEncoder` | 🐢 Slow (50ms) | Best | ❌ | Cloud | | `CohereEncoder` | 🐢 Slow (40ms) | Best | ❌ | Cloud | For a routing system, **speed matters more than marginal accuracy**. Use `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`. --- ## 9. RouteLLM Integration RouteLLM uses trained ML classifiers to decide between a strong and weak model. ```python # pip install routellm from routellm.controller import Controller # Initialize with your models client = Controller( routers=["mf"], # Matrix factorization classifier strong_model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514", weak_model="ollama/llama3.3:8b", ) # Use like a normal OpenAI client response = client.chat.completions.create( model="router-mf-0.11593", # "mf" router, threshold 0.11593 messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2?"}] ) # → Routed to weak model (llama3.3:8b) response = client.chat.completions.create( model="router-mf-0.11593", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Design a microservices architecture for an e-commerce platform"}] ) # → Routed to strong model (claude-sonnet-4) ``` ### Cost Savings RouteLLM reports these savings on common benchmarks: | Threshold | Strong Model % | Quality Loss | Cost Savings | |-----------|---------------|-------------|-------------| | 0.5 | ~15% routed to strong | <1% | ~85% | | 0.3 | ~30% routed to strong | <2% | ~70% | | 0.1 | ~50% routed to strong | <3% | ~50% | | 0.05 | ~70% routed to strong | <5% | ~30% | The sweet spot is usually threshold 0.1-0.3, giving **40-70% cost savings** with minimal quality impact. --- ## 10. Logging & Observability ### SQLite Schema for Routing Decisions ```sql CREATE TABLE routing_decisions ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, timestamp TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now')), -- Request info message_hash TEXT NOT NULL, -- SHA256 of message (privacy) message_length INTEGER NOT NULL, -- Routing result layer TEXT NOT NULL, -- "rules", "semantic", "llm" model_tier TEXT NOT NULL, -- "simple", "coding", "reasoning", "creative" confidence REAL NOT NULL, reason TEXT, privacy_override BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE, -- Performance routing_latency_ms REAL NOT NULL, -- Time to classify model_latency_ms REAL, -- Time for model response -- Cost input_tokens INTEGER, output_tokens INTEGER, cost_usd REAL, -- Quality user_feedback TEXT -- "good", "bad", NULL ); -- Indexes for common queries CREATE INDEX idx_timestamp ON routing_decisions(timestamp); CREATE INDEX idx_model_tier ON routing_decisions(model_tier); CREATE INDEX idx_layer ON routing_decisions(layer); ``` ### Useful Queries ```sql -- Distribution by tier (last 24 hours) SELECT model_tier, COUNT(*) as count, ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER(), 1) as pct FROM routing_decisions WHERE timestamp > datetime('now', '-1 day') GROUP BY model_tier ORDER BY count DESC; -- Average latency by layer SELECT layer, ROUND(AVG(routing_latency_ms), 1) as avg_routing_ms, ROUND(AVG(model_latency_ms), 1) as avg_model_ms FROM routing_decisions GROUP BY layer; -- Daily cost tracking SELECT DATE(timestamp) as date, SUM(cost_usd) as daily_cost, COUNT(*) as requests, ROUND(SUM(cost_usd) / COUNT(*) * 1000, 4) as cost_per_request_mills FROM routing_decisions GROUP BY DATE(timestamp) ORDER BY date DESC LIMIT 7; -- Privacy override frequency SELECT DATE(timestamp) as date, SUM(CASE WHEN privacy_override THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as overrides, COUNT(*) as total, ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN privacy_override THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 1) as override_pct FROM routing_decisions GROUP BY DATE(timestamp); ``` --- ## 11. Config Pattern: Layered YAML The routing system uses a layered config that merges defaults → user config → env vars. ```python import os import yaml from pathlib import Path from typing import Any def deep_merge(base: dict, override: dict) -> dict: """Recursively merge override into base. Override wins on conflicts.""" result = base.copy() for key, value in override.items(): if key in result and isinstance(result[key], dict) and isinstance(value, dict): result[key] = deep_merge(result[key], value) else: result[key] = value return result def resolve_env_vars(config: dict) -> dict: """Replace ${ENV_VAR} and os.environ/ENV_VAR patterns with actual values.""" result = {} for key, value in config.items(): if isinstance(value, dict): result[key] = resolve_env_vars(value) elif isinstance(value, str): # Handle ${ENV_VAR} pattern if value.startswith("${") and value.endswith("}"): env_key = value[2:-1] result[key] = os.environ.get(env_key, value) # Handle os.environ/ENV_VAR pattern (LiteLLM style) elif value.startswith("os.environ/"): env_key = value.split("/", 1)[1] result[key] = os.environ.get(env_key, value) else: result[key] = value else: result[key] = value return result def load_config( defaults_path: Path = Path("config/defaults.yaml"), user_path: Path = Path("~/.config/router/config.yaml"), env_prefix: str = "ROUTER_" ) -> dict: """Load configuration with layered precedence: defaults → user → env.""" # Layer 1: Defaults with open(defaults_path) as f: config = yaml.safe_load(f) or {} # Layer 2: User config (if exists) user_path = user_path.expanduser() if user_path.exists(): with open(user_path) as f: user_config = yaml.safe_load(f) or {} config = deep_merge(config, user_config) # Layer 3: Environment variables # ROUTER_MODEL__DEFAULT=gpt-4o → model.default = "gpt-4o" for key, value in os.environ.items(): if key.startswith(env_prefix): config_key = key[len(env_prefix):].lower() parts = config_key.split("__") # Double underscore = nesting current = config for part in parts[:-1]: current = current.setdefault(part, {}) current[parts[-1]] = value # Resolve environment variable references config = resolve_env_vars(config) return config ``` --- ## 12. Cost Analysis ### Monthly Cost Comparison (10,000 requests/month) | Scenario | Avg Cost/Request | Monthly Cost | Savings | |----------|-----------------|-------------|---------| | All GPT-4o | $0.025 | $250 | — | | All Claude Sonnet 4 | $0.020 | $200 | 20% | | Tiered (no local) | $0.010 | $100 | 60% | | Tiered (with local) | $0.005 | $50 | **80%** | | RouteLLM (threshold 0.15) | $0.004 | $40 | **84%** | ### Break-Even Analysis Setting up routing has upfront costs: - **Development**: ~8-16 hours - **Infrastructure**: Local embedding model (~80MB RAM), SQLite (negligible) - **Maintenance**: ~1 hour/month to review routing accuracy At $200/month savings, the break-even point is approximately **1-2 months**. --- ## 13. Gotchas & Lessons Learned ### 🚨 Gotcha #1: Lazy-Init the Semantic Encoder **Problem:** `HuggingFaceEncoder` downloads and loads the model on first instantiation. If you do this at import time, your application startup takes 3-5 seconds even if the semantic router isn't needed. **Before (slow startup):** ```python # BAD: Loads model at import time class SemanticRouter: def __init__(self): self.encoder = HuggingFaceEncoder( # 3-5 second delay! model_name="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2" ) self.route_layer = RouteLayer(encoder=self.encoder, routes=self.routes) ``` **After (lazy loading):** ```python # GOOD: Loads model only when first used class SemanticRouter: def __init__(self): self._encoder = None self._route_layer = None @property def encoder(self): if self._encoder is None: self._encoder = HuggingFaceEncoder( model_name="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2" ) return self._encoder ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #2: LLM Classifier Must Never Return None **Problem:** If the LLM classifier returns `None`, the entire routing pipeline fails with an unhandled error. This happens when: - The API is down - The response can't be parsed as JSON - The model returns an unexpected category - Rate limiting kicks in **Solution:** Wrap everything in try/except and always return a default: ```python def route(self, message: str) -> RouteDecision: try: # ... classification logic ... return RouteDecision(model_tier=category, ...) except Exception as e: # ALWAYS return something return RouteDecision( model_tier="coding", # Safe default confidence=0.3, reason=f"Classifier error: {e}", privacy_override=False ) ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #3: Cache Routing Decisions **Problem:** Identical requests within a short window hit all three layers redundantly. **Solution:** Add a TTL cache for routing decisions (not model responses — that's LiteLLM's job): ```python from functools import lru_cache import hashlib @lru_cache(maxsize=1000) def _cached_route(message_hash: str, message: str) -> RouteDecision: return self._route_uncached(message) def route(self, message: str) -> RouteDecision: msg_hash = hashlib.sha256(message.encode()).hexdigest()[:16] return _cached_route(msg_hash, message) ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #4: Monitor Tier Distribution **Problem:** After deploying routing, you may find that 90%+ of traffic goes to one tier. This usually means your rules or utterances are too broad/narrow. **Solution:** Set up an alert if any tier handles >70% or <5% of traffic: ```python def check_distribution(db_path: str, hours: int = 24) -> dict: """Check if routing distribution is healthy.""" conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path) rows = conn.execute(""" SELECT model_tier, COUNT(*) as n FROM routing_decisions WHERE timestamp > datetime('now', ? || ' hours') GROUP BY model_tier """, (f"-{hours}",)).fetchall() total = sum(r[1] for r in rows) dist = {r[0]: r[1] / total * 100 for r in rows} for tier, pct in dist.items(): if pct > 70: print(f"⚠️ WARNING: {tier} tier handling {pct:.1f}% of traffic") if pct < 5: print(f"⚠️ WARNING: {tier} tier handling only {pct:.1f}% of traffic") return dist ``` --- ## 14. Quick Reference ### Layer Summary | Layer | Latency | Coverage | Method | Library | |-------|---------|----------|--------|---------| | Rules | 0ms | ~60% | Regex patterns | Built-in | | Semantic | 5-20ms | ~30% | Embedding similarity | semantic-router | | LLM | 100-300ms | ~10% | ML classification | RouteLLM / LiteLLM | | Privacy | 0ms | 100% (post-check) | Pattern matching | Built-in | ### Model Tier → Model Mapping | Tier | Primary | Fallback | Use Case | |------|---------|----------|----------| | simple | Ollama/Llama 3.3 8B | — | Greetings, Q&A, translations | | coding | Claude Sonnet 4 | GPT-4o | Code gen, debugging, DevOps | | reasoning | o3 | Gemini 2.5 Pro | Architecture, analysis, planning | | creative | GPT-4o | Claude Sonnet 4 | Writing, docs, content | ### Essential Commands ```bash # Start LiteLLM proxy litellm --config litellm_config.yaml --port 4000 # Test routing curl http://localhost:4000/v1/chat/completions \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"model": "coding", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}]}' # Check routing stats (if using SQLite logging) sqlite3 routing.db "SELECT model_tier, COUNT(*) FROM routing_decisions GROUP BY model_tier;" ``` --- *Last updated: June 2026 | Author: Joel Thomas*
