args.CodeContent: # WSL2 Automation & Installer Scripts > A comprehensive guide to automating WSL2 setup, service management, Docker/GPU configuration, and idempotent installer patterns for AI workstations. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [Creating Named WSL Distros](#creating-named-wsl-distros) 2. [Managing WSL Instances](#managing-wsl-instances) 3. [/etc/wsl.conf — Complete Reference](#wslconf-reference) 4. [Installing Docker Engine (Without Docker Desktop)](#docker-engine) 5. [NVIDIA Container Toolkit for GPU Passthrough](#nvidia-gpu) 6. [systemd Services in WSL2](#systemd-services) 7. [Creating systemd User Services](#user-services) 8. [Idempotent Installer Pattern](#idempotent-installer) 9. [Port Forwarding & Networking](#port-forwarding) 10. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 11. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. Creating Named WSL Distros WSL2 supports multiple named instances of the same distribution. This is essential for isolating environments (e.g., one for AI workloads, one for web dev). ### Install a Named Distro ```powershell # List available distros wsl --list --online # Install with a custom name wsl --install Ubuntu-24.04 --name agentos # Or with Ubuntu 26.04 (if available) wsl --install Ubuntu-26.04 --name agentos ``` ### Full Setup Workflow ```powershell # Step 1: Install the distro wsl --install Ubuntu-24.04 --name agentos # Step 2: Set as default (optional) wsl --set-default agentos # Step 3: Enter the distro wsl -d agentos # Step 4: Set up your user (first run prompts for this) # Username: joel # Password: (your choice) # Step 5: Update packages sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y ``` ### Import/Export for Backup ```powershell # Export a distro to a tar file (backup) wsl --export agentos D:\backups\agentos-backup.tar # Import a distro from a tar file (restore) wsl --import agentos-restored D:\wsl\agentos-restored D:\backups\agentos-backup.tar # This creates a full clone with all installed packages and configs ``` --- ## 2. Managing WSL Instances ### Listing Distros ```powershell # List all installed distros with status and WSL version wsl --list --verbose # Example output: # NAME STATE VERSION # * agentos Running 2 # Ubuntu Stopped 2 # docker-ce Running 2 ``` ### Running Commands Without Entering WSL ```powershell # Run a single command in a specific distro wsl -d agentos -- echo "Hello from WSL" # Run a bash script wsl -d agentos -- bash -c "cd /home/joel && python3 script.py" # Run as a specific user wsl -d agentos -u root -- apt update # Check if a service is running wsl -d agentos -- systemctl is-active ollama ``` ### Lifecycle Management ```powershell # Shut down a specific distro wsl --terminate agentos # Shut down ALL distros and the WSL VM wsl --shutdown # Unregister (delete) a distro — DESTRUCTIVE wsl --unregister old-distro # Set WSL version for a distro wsl --set-version agentos 2 ``` ### Resource Management Create or edit `%USERPROFILE%\.wslconfig` to control WSL2 VM resources: ```ini # C:\Users\joel\.wslconfig [wsl2] # Limit memory (default: 50% of host RAM) memory=8GB # Limit CPU cores (default: all) processors=4 # Limit swap swap=4GB # Swap file location swapFile=D:\\wsl\\swap.vhdx # Enable GPU support gpuSupport=true # Disable page reporting (helps with memory management) pageReporting=false # Network mode networkingMode=mirrored ``` > **⚠️ Note:** `.wslconfig` is a **Windows-side** file that applies to ALL distros. `wsl.conf` is a **Linux-side** file that applies per-distro. --- ## 3. /etc/wsl.conf — Complete Reference This is the per-distro configuration file inside WSL. Every section matters. ```ini # /etc/wsl.conf — Per-distro WSL configuration [boot] # Enable systemd — CRITICAL for Docker, Ollama, and other services # Without this, `systemctl` commands won't work systemd=true # Optional: Run a command on boot # command=/usr/local/bin/startup.sh [automount] # Auto-mount Windows drives under /mnt/ enabled=true # Mount options — CRITICAL for proper file permissions # metadata: Enables Linux file permissions on Windows drives # umask=0022: Default permissions for files (owner read/write, others read) # fmask=0011: Default permissions for files specifically options="metadata,umask=0022,fmask=0011" # Don't process /etc/fstab mountFsTab=false # Mount point for Windows drives root=/mnt/ [interop] # Allow running Windows executables from WSL (e.g., code.exe, explorer.exe) enabled=true # Add Windows PATH to WSL PATH (useful for calling Windows tools) appendWindowsPath=true [network] # Auto-generate /etc/resolv.conf generateResolvConf=true # Set hostname hostname=agentos # Auto-generate /etc/hosts generateHosts=true [user] # Default user when launching WSL default=joel ``` ### Applying Changes ```powershell # After editing /etc/wsl.conf, you MUST restart WSL # From PowerShell (NOT from within WSL): wsl --shutdown # Wait a few seconds, then re-enter wsl -d agentos # Verify systemd is running systemctl --version ``` > **🚨 Critical:** Closing the terminal window is NOT enough. You must run `wsl --shutdown` to fully restart the WSL2 VM. The wsl.conf is only read during VM boot. --- ## 4. Installing Docker Engine (Without Docker Desktop) Docker Desktop requires a license for commercial use. Docker Engine inside WSL is free and often more performant. ### Step-by-Step Installation ```bash #!/bin/bash # install-docker.sh — Install Docker Engine in WSL2 set -euo pipefail echo "=== Installing Docker Engine ===" # Remove old versions sudo apt-get remove -y docker docker-engine docker.io containerd runc 2>/dev/null || true # Install prerequisites sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y \ ca-certificates \ curl \ gnupg \ lsb-release # Add Docker's official GPG key sudo install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | \ sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg sudo chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg # Set up the repository echo \ "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg] \ https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \ $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" | \ sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null # Install Docker Engine sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y \ docker-ce \ docker-ce-cli \ containerd.io \ docker-buildx-plugin \ docker-compose-plugin # Add current user to docker group (avoids sudo) sudo usermod -aG docker $USER # Enable and start Docker service sudo systemctl enable docker sudo systemctl start docker echo "=== Docker Engine installed ===" echo "⚠️ Log out and back in for docker group membership to take effect." echo "Test with: docker run hello-world" ``` ### Verification ```bash # Check Docker is running sudo systemctl status docker # Test without sudo (after re-login) docker run --rm hello-world # Check Docker Compose docker compose version ``` --- ## 5. NVIDIA Container Toolkit for GPU Passthrough WSL2 supports GPU passthrough for CUDA workloads. The NVIDIA Container Toolkit allows Docker containers to access the GPU. ### Prerequisites 1. **Windows NVIDIA driver** installed (NOT the Linux driver — WSL uses Windows drivers) 2. **WSL2** (not WSL1) 3. **GPU support enabled** in `.wslconfig` ### Installation ```bash #!/bin/bash # install-nvidia-toolkit.sh — NVIDIA Container Toolkit for WSL2 set -euo pipefail echo "=== Installing NVIDIA Container Toolkit ===" # Verify GPU is visible in WSL if ! command -v nvidia-smi &> /dev/null; then echo "❌ nvidia-smi not found. Ensure Windows NVIDIA drivers are installed." exit 1 fi echo "GPU detected:" nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,driver_version,memory.total --format=csv,noheader # Add NVIDIA Container Toolkit repository curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | \ sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/stable/deb/nvidia-container-toolkit.list | \ sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' | \ sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list # Install the toolkit sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit # Configure Docker to use NVIDIA runtime sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker # Restart Docker sudo systemctl restart docker echo "=== NVIDIA Container Toolkit installed ===" echo "Test with: docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.0-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi" ``` ### Verification ```bash # Run a GPU test container docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.0-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi # Expected output shows your GPU inside the container: # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ # | NVIDIA-SMI 555.99 Driver Version: 555.99 CUDA Version: 12.4 | # | GPU Name ... | # | NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4060 | # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ # Run Ollama with GPU support docker run -d --gpus all \ -v ollama:/root/.ollama \ -p 11434:11434 \ --name ollama \ ollama/ollama ``` --- ## 6. systemd Services in WSL2 ### Enabling systemd systemd support requires WSL 0.67.6+ and must be explicitly enabled: ```ini # /etc/wsl.conf [boot] systemd=true ``` After setting this, `wsl --shutdown` and restart. ### Verifying systemd ```bash # Check if systemd is PID 1 ps -p 1 -o comm= # Should output: systemd # Check systemctl works systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running # If you see "System has not been booted with systemd", # wsl.conf wasn't applied — run wsl --shutdown and try again ``` ### System vs User Services | Type | Location | Runs as | Starts at | |------|----------|---------|-----------| | System service | `/etc/systemd/system/` | root | Boot | | User service | `~/.config/systemd/user/` | Current user | Login | For AI services (Ollama, LiteLLM, n8n), **system services** are usually better because they start at boot and run regardless of who's logged in. --- ## 7. Creating systemd User Services ### Ollama Service ```ini # /etc/systemd/system/ollama.service [Unit] Description=Ollama LLM Server After=network-online.target Wants=network-online.target [Service] Type=simple User=joel Group=joel ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/ollama serve Restart=always RestartSec=5 # Environment Environment="OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434" Environment="OLLAMA_MODELS=/home/joel/.ollama/models" # Resource limits LimitNOFILE=65535 LimitMEMLOCK=infinity # GPU access Environment="CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0" [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target ``` ### LiteLLM Proxy Service ```ini # /etc/systemd/system/litellm.service [Unit] Description=LiteLLM Proxy Server After=network-online.target ollama.service Wants=network-online.target [Service] Type=simple User=joel Group=joel WorkingDirectory=/home/joel/litellm ExecStart=/home/joel/.local/bin/litellm \ --config /home/joel/litellm/config.yaml \ --port 4000 \ --host 0.0.0.0 Restart=always RestartSec=5 # Load API keys from environment file EnvironmentFile=/home/joel/litellm/.env [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target ``` ### n8n Workflow Service ```ini # /etc/systemd/system/n8n.service [Unit] Description=n8n Workflow Automation After=network-online.target Wants=network-online.target [Service] Type=simple User=joel Group=joel ExecStart=/usr/bin/n8n start Restart=always RestartSec=5 # n8n environment Environment="N8N_PORT=5678" Environment="N8N_PROTOCOL=http" Environment="N8N_HOST=0.0.0.0" Environment="GENERIC_TIMEZONE=Asia/Kolkata" Environment="N8N_DEFAULT_BINARY_DATA_MODE=filesystem" Environment="N8N_USER_FOLDER=/home/joel/.n8n" # Database (SQLite by default) Environment="DB_TYPE=sqlite" Environment="DB_SQLITE_DATABASE=/home/joel/.n8n/database.sqlite" [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target ``` ### Enabling and Managing Services ```bash # Reload systemd after adding/changing service files sudo systemctl daemon-reload # Enable (start on boot) and start services sudo systemctl enable --now ollama sudo systemctl enable --now litellm sudo systemctl enable --now n8n # Check status sudo systemctl status ollama sudo systemctl status litellm sudo systemctl status n8n # View logs journalctl -u ollama -f # Follow logs journalctl -u litellm --since today # Today's logs journalctl -u n8n -n 50 # Last 50 lines # Restart a service sudo systemctl restart ollama # Stop a service sudo systemctl stop litellm ``` --- ## 8. Idempotent Installer Pattern When automating WSL setup, scripts should be **idempotent** — safe to run multiple times without side effects. ### The Marker File Pattern ```bash #!/bin/bash # install-agentos.sh — Idempotent installer for AgentOS # Safe to run multiple times. Uses marker files to skip completed steps. set -euo pipefail MARKER_DIR="/home/joel/.install-markers" mkdir -p "$MARKER_DIR" # ── Helper Functions ────────────────────────────────────────────── log() { echo "[$(date '+%H:%M:%S')] $*" } # Run a step only if its marker doesn't exist run_step() { local step_name="$1" local marker_file="$MARKER_DIR/$step_name.done" shift if [[ -f "$marker_file" ]]; then log "⏭️ Skipping '$step_name' (already done)" return 0 fi log "🔧 Running '$step_name'..." # Run the step function if "$@"; then # Mark as done date > "$marker_file" log "✅ Completed '$step_name'" else log "❌ Failed '$step_name' (exit code: $?)" return 1 fi } # ── Installation Steps ─────────────────────────────────────────── step_system_update() { sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get upgrade -y sudo apt-get install -y \ build-essential \ curl \ wget \ git \ unzip \ jq \ htop } step_install_python() { sudo apt-get install -y python3 python3-pip python3-venv python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip } step_install_node() { # Install Node.js via nvm curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.0/install.sh | bash export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm" # shellcheck source=/dev/null [ -s "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh" ] && \. "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh" nvm install 22 nvm alias default 22 } step_install_ollama() { curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh sudo systemctl enable ollama sudo systemctl start ollama # Pull a default model ollama pull llama3.3:8b } step_install_docker() { # (Full Docker install script from Section 4) bash /home/joel/scripts/install-docker.sh } step_install_nvidia_toolkit() { bash /home/joel/scripts/install-nvidia-toolkit.sh } step_install_litellm() { pip install litellm[proxy] mkdir -p /home/joel/litellm # Copy config if it doesn't exist if [[ ! -f /home/joel/litellm/config.yaml ]]; then cp /home/joel/templates/litellm-config.yaml /home/joel/litellm/config.yaml fi } step_install_n8n() { npm install -g n8n mkdir -p /home/joel/.n8n } step_configure_services() { # Copy service files sudo cp /home/joel/services/*.service /etc/systemd/system/ sudo systemctl daemon-reload sudo systemctl enable ollama litellm n8n } # ── Main ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── main() { log "=== AgentOS Installer ===" log "Marker directory: $MARKER_DIR" echo "" run_step "system-update" step_system_update run_step "python" step_install_python run_step "nodejs" step_install_node run_step "docker" step_install_docker run_step "nvidia-toolkit" step_install_nvidia_toolkit run_step "ollama" step_install_ollama run_step "litellm" step_install_litellm run_step "n8n" step_install_n8n run_step "configure-services" step_configure_services echo "" log "=== Installation complete ===" log "Markers saved in: $MARKER_DIR" log "To re-run a step, delete its marker file." } main "$@" ``` ### Resetting a Step ```bash # Re-run the "ollama" step rm ~/.install-markers/ollama.done bash install-agentos.sh # Re-run everything rm -rf ~/.install-markers/ bash install-agentos.sh ``` --- ## 9. Port Forwarding & Networking ### How WSL2 Networking Works WSL2 runs as a lightweight VM with its own network adapter. However, **ports are automatically forwarded** from WSL to Windows localhost. ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Windows Host │ │ │ │ Browser → http://localhost:5678 ────┐ │ │ Browser → http://localhost:4000 ──┐ │ │ │ Browser → http://localhost:11434 ┐│ │ │ │ ││ │ │ │ ┌────────────────────────────────┤├─┤──│──────────┐ │ │ WSL2 VM ││ │ │ │ │ │ ││ │ │ │ │ │ Ollama ← :11434 ◄───────────┘│ │ │ │ │ │ LiteLLM ← :4000 ◄────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ n8n ← :5678 ◄──────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Key Points 1. **Automatic localhost forwarding:** Services in WSL2 bound to `0.0.0.0` or `127.0.0.1` are accessible from Windows via `localhost`. No manual port forwarding needed. 2. **Bind to 0.0.0.0:** Services should bind to `0.0.0.0`, not `127.0.0.1`, to be accessible from Windows: ```bash # ✅ Accessible from Windows ollama serve --host 0.0.0.0:11434 # ❌ Only accessible within WSL ollama serve --host 127.0.0.1:11434 ``` 3. **Port conflicts:** If a Windows service uses the same port, WSL won't be able to bind to it. Check for conflicts: ```powershell # Windows: Check what's using a port netstat -ano | findstr :5678 # WSL: Check what's using a port sudo ss -tlnp | grep :5678 ``` ### Standard Port Assignments | Service | Port | Purpose | |---------|------|---------| | Ollama | 11434 | LLM inference server | | LiteLLM | 4000 | LLM proxy/router | | n8n | 5678 | Workflow automation | | Hermes Dashboard | 9119 | Agent monitoring | | PostgreSQL | 5432 | Database | | Redis | 6379 | Cache | ### Accessing WSL from Other Machines By default, WSL services are only accessible from the Windows host. To expose to the network: ```powershell # PowerShell (Admin): Forward a port to the network netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 ` listenport=5678 ` listenaddress=0.0.0.0 ` connectport=5678 ` connectaddress=127.0.0.1 # Don't forget Windows Firewall New-NetFirewallRule ` -DisplayName "n8n from network" ` -Direction Inbound ` -LocalPort 5678 ` -Protocol TCP ` -Action Allow # Remove the proxy when done netsh interface portproxy delete v4tov4 ` listenport=5678 ` listenaddress=0.0.0.0 ``` --- ## 10. Gotchas & Lessons Learned ### 🚨 Gotcha #1: Unicode Encoding in WSL CLI Output **Problem:** When calling `wsl --list --verbose` from PowerShell, the output contains null bytes between characters (UTF-16LE encoding). This breaks string parsing: ```powershell # This looks normal but is actually UTF-16LE: $output = wsl --list --verbose $output # Displays fine in terminal $output.Length # Returns double the expected length! ``` **The actual bytes:** ``` U 00 b 00 u 00 n 00 t 00 u 00 ← "Ubuntu" in UTF-16LE ``` **Solution — Decode UTF-16LE:** ```powershell # Method 1: Use [Console]::OutputEncoding [Console]::OutputEncoding = [System.Text.Encoding]::Unicode $output = wsl --list --verbose # Method 2: Manual decode $rawOutput = wsl --list --verbose 2>&1 $cleanOutput = $rawOutput -replace '\x00', '' # Method 3: Use --quiet flag where available $distros = wsl --list --quiet # Simpler output, fewer encoding issues # Method 4: Robust parsing function function Get-WslDistros { $raw = (wsl --list --verbose 2>&1) -replace '\x00', '' $lines = $raw -split "`n" | Where-Object { $_ -match '\S' } | Select-Object -Skip 1 $distros = foreach ($line in $lines) { if ($line -match '^\s*\*?\s*(\S+)\s+(Running|Stopped)\s+(\d+)') { [PSCustomObject]@{ Name = $Matches[1] State = $Matches[2] Version = $Matches[3] Default = $line.TrimStart().StartsWith('*') } } } return $distros } # Usage: $distros = Get-WslDistros $distros | Format-Table ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #2: `wsl --shutdown` Required After wsl.conf Changes **Problem:** Editing `/etc/wsl.conf` has no effect until the WSL VM is fully restarted. Simply closing the terminal doesn't restart the VM. **The correct workflow:** ```powershell # Step 1: Edit wsl.conf (from WSL) wsl -d agentos -- sudo nano /etc/wsl.conf # Step 2: Shut down ALL WSL instances (from PowerShell) wsl --shutdown # Step 3: Wait 3-5 seconds for full shutdown Start-Sleep -Seconds 5 # Step 4: Restart your distro wsl -d agentos # Step 5: Verify the changes took effect wsl -d agentos -- ps -p 1 -o comm= # Should output: systemd (if you enabled systemd) ``` **Common mistake:** Running `wsl --terminate agentos` only stops that specific distro, but the WSL VM might still be running (if other distros are active). Use `wsl --shutdown` to restart the entire VM, which is required for wsl.conf changes. ### 🚨 Gotcha #3: File Permission Issues on Windows Mounts **Problem:** By default, all files on Windows drives (`/mnt/c/`, `/mnt/d/`) have `777` permissions, making `chmod` a no-op. Git will show every file as modified. **Solution:** Add metadata mount options to wsl.conf: ```ini [automount] options="metadata,umask=0022,fmask=0011" ``` After restart: ```bash # Now chmod works on /mnt/ drives chmod 600 /mnt/c/Users/joel/.ssh/id_rsa ls -la /mnt/c/Users/joel/.ssh/id_rsa # -rw------- 1 joel joel 2602 Jun 14 12:00 id_rsa ✅ ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #4: systemd Service Ordering **Problem:** Services may start before their dependencies are ready. For example, LiteLLM starts before Ollama is listening, causing connection failures. **Solution:** Use `After=` and add health checks: ```ini [Unit] Description=LiteLLM Proxy After=network-online.target ollama.service Wants=network-online.target [Service] # Wait for Ollama to be ready ExecStartPre=/bin/bash -c 'for i in $(seq 1 30); do curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/tags > /dev/null && exit 0; sleep 1; done; exit 1' ExecStart=/home/joel/.local/bin/litellm --config /home/joel/litellm/config.yaml --port 4000 ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #5: Disk Space for Models **Problem:** LLM models are large. A single 70B model can be 40GB+. The default WSL virtual disk is on C: drive which may be small. **Solution:** Move the WSL disk to a larger drive: ```powershell # Export the distro wsl --export agentos D:\temp\agentos.tar # Unregister (delete from default location) wsl --unregister agentos # Import to a new location wsl --import agentos D:\wsl\agentos D:\temp\agentos.tar # Clean up Remove-Item D:\temp\agentos.tar ``` Or symlink the Ollama models directory: ```bash # Move models to a larger drive (if mounted) sudo mv /home/joel/.ollama/models /mnt/d/ollama-models ln -s /mnt/d/ollama-models /home/joel/.ollama/models ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #6: DNS Resolution Failures **Problem:** WSL2 sometimes fails to resolve DNS after sleep/wake or network changes. **Solution:** ```bash # Quick fix: manually set DNS echo "nameserver 8.8.8.8" | sudo tee /etc/resolv.conf # Permanent fix: prevent WSL from overwriting resolv.conf # In /etc/wsl.conf: [network] generateResolvConf=false # Then create a persistent resolv.conf: sudo rm /etc/resolv.conf sudo bash -c 'echo "nameserver 8.8.8.8" > /etc/resolv.conf' sudo bash -c 'echo "nameserver 8.8.4.4" >> /etc/resolv.conf' sudo chattr +i /etc/resolv.conf # Make immutable ``` --- ## 11. Quick Reference ### Essential PowerShell Commands ```powershell wsl --list --verbose # List all distros wsl --shutdown # Restart WSL VM wsl -d agentos # Enter a distro wsl -d agentos -- # Run command in distro wsl --export agentos file.tar # Backup wsl --import name dir file.tar # Restore wsl --terminate agentos # Stop one distro wsl --unregister old-distro # Delete (destructive!) ``` ### Essential WSL Bash Commands ```bash systemctl status # Check service journalctl -u -f # Follow logs sudo systemctl daemon-reload # After .service file changes sudo systemctl enable --now X # Enable + start nvidia-smi # Check GPU docker info # Check Docker ``` ### Config File Locations | File | Location | Scope | |------|----------|-------| | `.wslconfig` | `%USERPROFILE%\.wslconfig` | All distros (Windows-side) | | `wsl.conf` | `/etc/wsl.conf` | Per-distro (Linux-side) | | Ollama service | `/etc/systemd/system/ollama.service` | System service | | LiteLLM service | `/etc/systemd/system/litellm.service` | System service | | n8n service | `/etc/systemd/system/n8n.service` | System service | | Install markers | `~/.install-markers/` | Idempotent installer state | ### Port Map | Port | Service | Protocol | |------|---------|----------| | 4000 | LiteLLM Proxy | HTTP | | 5432 | PostgreSQL | TCP | | 5678 | n8n | HTTP | | 6379 | Redis | TCP | | 9119 | Hermes Dashboard | HTTP | | 11434 | Ollama | HTTP | --- *Last updated: June 2026 | Author: Joel Thomas*

args.CodeContent: # Developer Personal Branding (India, 2026) > A comprehensive playbook for Indian CS students targeting top-tier placements — covering competitive positioning, online presence, content strategy, and actionable career acceleration tactics. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [VIT Chennai Placement Tiers](#placement-tiers) 2. [Competitive Positioning Analysis](#competitive-positioning) 3. [Gap Analysis & Action Plans](#gap-analysis) 4. [LinkedIn Optimization](#linkedin) 5. [GitHub Profile Engineering](#github) 6. [Blogging & Content Strategy](#blogging) 7. [Hackathon Strategy](#hackathons) 8. [Cold Email Templates](#cold-emails) 9. [Content Formats That Work](#content-formats) 10. [Indian AI Market Landscape (2026)](#ai-market) 11. [Certification ROI Analysis](#cert-roi) 12. [Files to Maintain](#files-to-maintain) 13. [Monthly Execution Checklist](#monthly-checklist) 14. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. VIT Chennai Placement Tiers VIT Chennai follows a structured placement system with distinct tiers: | Tier | Package (LPA) | Typical Companies | Seats | Selection Rate | |------|--------------|-------------------|-------|---------------| | **Marquee** | ≥₹20 LPA | Google, Microsoft, Goldman Sachs, Tower Research, DE Shaw | 5-15 | ~2% | | **Super Dream** | ≥₹10 LPA | Amazon, Flipkart, Walmart, Intuit, SAP Labs, Adobe | 30-60 | ~8% | | **Dream** | ≥₹6 LPA | TCS Digital, Infosys Power Programmer, Cognizant GenC Pro | 100-200 | ~20% | | **Regular** | ₹3-6 LPA | Wipro, Infosys, TCS, Capgemini, HCL | 400+ | ~60% | ### What Each Tier Looks For **Marquee (₹20+ LPA):** - Strong DSA (LeetCode hard in 30 min) - System design fundamentals - Research/publications (huge differentiator) - Open-source contributions - Unique projects (not TODO apps) **Super Dream (₹10-20 LPA):** - Solid DSA (LeetCode medium consistently) - Good project portfolio (3-4 meaningful projects) - Internship experience - Clear communication skills - Domain knowledge (ML, web, systems) **Dream (₹6-10 LPA):** - Basic DSA proficiency - Academic projects with some depth - Decent CGPA (typically ≥8.0) - Aptitude test performance ### Timeline for VIT 2027 Batch ``` June 2026 ← YOU ARE HERE ├── Start cert prep (AWS) ├── Finalize portfolio site └── Begin internship applications July-Aug 2026 ├── Complete AWS cert ├── Start RAG project └── Apply to hiring hackathons Sep-Oct 2026 ├── Campus placement prep begins ├── LeetCode 200+ problems └── System design study Nov 2026 - Feb 2027 ├── Marquee companies visit ├── Super Dream companies visit └── Interview season ``` --- ## 2. Competitive Positioning Analysis ### Joel's Current Assets | Asset | Details | Competitive Value | |-------|---------|-------------------| | **Published Research** | IEEE paper on federated learning | 🔥🔥🔥 Top 5% of applicants | | **4 Certifications** | Google Cloud, AWS, Meta, etc. | 🔥🔥 Top 15% | | **8 Programming Languages** | Python, TS, Rust, Go, C++, Java, SQL, Bash | 🔥🔥 Breadth signal | | **Creative Portfolio** | ThinkPad terminal theme, Astro + React | 🔥🔥🔥 Memorable | | **AI/ML Focus** | LLM routing, agent systems, federated learning | 🔥🔥🔥 High-demand domain | ### Positioning Statement ``` Joel Thomas — AI/ML Engineer building intelligent systems with LLMs, federated learning, and full-stack web technologies. Published IEEE researcher. VIT Chennai '27. ``` ### Competitive Moat Analysis ``` Joel's Positioning Map Research Depth ▲ High │ ★ Joel (research + code + creative) │ │ ○ Typical top student (code only) │ Med │ ○ Good intern (experience, no research) │ │ ○ Average student (projects only) Low │ └──────────────────────────────────────► Low Med High Project Creativity ``` Joel's unique advantage: **Research × Creativity**. Most students who publish research have bland portfolios. Most creative portfolios lack research depth. Joel has both. --- ## 3. Gap Analysis & Action Plans ### Gap #1: Cloud Certification (AWS) **Why it matters:** 85% of Marquee companies use AWS. An AWS cert signals production-readiness. | Detail | Value | |--------|-------| | **Target** | AWS Cloud Practitioner (CLF-C02) | | **Cost** | $100 (₹8,500) | | **Study Time** | 2 weeks, 2 hrs/day | | **ROI** | +₹1-2 LPA in offers | **Action Plan:** ``` Week 1: AWS fundamentals (EC2, S3, IAM, VPC, Lambda) - Resource: Stephane Maarek's Udemy course - Hands-on: Create a free-tier account, deploy a Lambda function Week 2: Practice exams + gaps - Resource: TutorialsDojo practice tests - Target: Score ≥85% on practice tests before booking exam - Book exam on Pearson VUE (online proctored) ``` ### Gap #2: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) Project **Why it matters:** RAG is the #1 production pattern for LLM applications in 2026. Companies ask about it in every ML interview. **Action Plan:** ``` Build: "Research Paper Q&A" — Upload papers, ask questions Stack: - Embedding: sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 - Vector DB: ChromaDB (simple) or Qdrant (production) - LLM: Ollama (local) via LiteLLM proxy - Framework: LangChain or LlamaIndex - Frontend: Streamlit or Next.js Timeline: 3 weekends Weekend 1: PDF parsing + embedding pipeline Weekend 2: Retrieval + LLM integration Weekend 3: Frontend + demo + README + GIF Deliverable: GitHub repo with: ☐ README with architecture diagram ☐ Demo GIF in README ☐ Docker Compose for one-command setup ☐ Blog post explaining the architecture ``` ### Gap #3: Internship Experience **Why it matters:** Internships demonstrate you can work in a team, handle real codebases, and deliver under constraints. **Action Plan:** ``` Path A: Traditional Internship (Preferred) - Target: AI/ML startups in Bangalore/Chennai - Platforms: Internshala, AngelList, LinkedIn - Timing: Apply July 2026, intern Aug-Oct 2026 - Compensation: ₹15-40K/month for good ones Path B: Open-Source Contribution (Backup) - Target: Major AI repos (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Hugging Face) - Start with "good first issue" tags - 3+ merged PRs = equivalent to internship on resume - Bonus: Open-source maintainer is a stronger signal than most internships Path C: Freelance/Contract (Backup) - Build something real for a local business or startup - Even unpaid work counts if it's a real product - Document everything — architecture, decisions, outcomes ``` --- ## 4. LinkedIn Optimization ### Headline Formula ``` [Role] | [Stack] | [Value Prop] | [Institution] ``` **Examples:** ``` ✅ AI/ML Engineer | PyTorch, LLMs, Federated Learning | Published IEEE Researcher | VIT Chennai '27 ✅ Full-Stack Developer | React, Node.js, AWS | Building AI-powered tools | VIT Chennai '27 ❌ Student at VIT Chennai (too generic) ❌ Aspiring Software Engineer (says nothing) ❌ Tech Enthusiast | Coder | Learner (cringe) ``` ### Profile Optimization Checklist ``` ☐ Professional headshot (not a selfie) ☐ Custom banner image (your project screenshot or a branded graphic) ☐ Headline using the formula above ☐ About section (3 paragraphs max): - Paragraph 1: Who you are + what you do - Paragraph 2: Key achievements (research, certs, projects) - Paragraph 3: What you're looking for ☐ Featured section: Pin your best 3 items - Portfolio website link - Research paper - Best project repo or blog post ☐ Experience section: Even student projects count — frame them well ☐ Skills section: List 10-15 relevant skills, get endorsements ☐ Recommendations: Request from professors, mentors, or project collaborators ``` ### About Section Template ```markdown 🔬 AI/ML researcher and full-stack developer with a published IEEE paper on federated learning and 4 industry certifications. Currently building: • Intelligent LLM routing systems (95% cost reduction via tiered model selection) • Creative portfolio experiences (interactive terminal with 3D CSS ThinkPad) • Workflow automation with n8n, LiteLLM, and agent architectures 🎓 B.Tech CSE @ VIT Chennai (2027) | 8 programming languages | 4 certifications | IEEE published researcher 📫 Looking for AI/ML internships and full-time roles starting 2027. Let's connect: joel@example.com ``` ### Post Strategy | Frequency | Content Type | Example | |-----------|-------------|---------| | 2x/week | Technical insight | "How I reduced LLM costs by 80% with model routing" | | 1x/week | Project update | "Building a ThinkPad-themed portfolio with Astro" | | 1x/month | Achievement post | "Published my first IEEE paper!" | | As needed | Article share | Share your Hashnode blog posts | --- ## 5. GitHub Profile Engineering ### Profile README Template Create a `README.md` in a repo named the same as your username: ```markdown ## Hey, I'm Joel 👋 **AI/ML Engineer** building intelligent systems with LLMs and federated learning. ```text 🔬 Published IEEE Researcher | 📜 4x Certified | 💻 8 Languages ``` ### 🛠️ What I'm Building - 🧠 **LLM Router** — Intelligent model routing saving 80% on API costs - 💻 **Portfolio** — Interactive ThinkPad terminal (Astro + React + xterm.js) - 📄 **Paper QA** — RAG system for research paper exploration ### 📊 Languages & Tools ![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Python-3776AB?logo=python&logoColor=white) ![TypeScript](https://img.shields.io/badge/-TypeScript-3178C6?logo=typescript&logoColor=white) ![Rust](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Rust-000?logo=rust&logoColor=white) ![React](https://img.shields.io/badge/-React-61DAFB?logo=react&logoColor=black) ![PyTorch](https://img.shields.io/badge/-PyTorch-EE4C2C?logo=pytorch&logoColor=white) ![Docker](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Docker-2496ED?logo=docker&logoColor=white) ![AWS](https://img.shields.io/badge/-AWS-FF9900?logo=amazonaws&logoColor=white) ### 📈 GitHub Stats ### 📫 Connect [![LinkedIn](https://img.shields.io/badge/-LinkedIn-0A66C2?logo=linkedin&logoColor=white)](https://linkedin.com/in/joelthomas) [![Portfolio](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Portfolio-00ff41?logo=terminal&logoColor=black)](https://joel.dev) [![Email](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Email-EA4335?logo=gmail&logoColor=white)](mailto:joel@example.com) ``` ### Pinned Repos (Choose 6) | Priority | Repo | Requirements | |----------|------|-------------| | 1 | **LLM Router** | README + architecture diagram + demo GIF | | 2 | **Portfolio** | README + screenshot + live link | | 3 | **RAG Project** | README + setup instructions + demo | | 4 | **Federated Learning** | README + paper link + results | | 5 | **Open-Source Contribution** | Link to your PRs | | 6 | **Best Course Project** | Clean code + good README | ### README Essentials for Every Repo Every pinned repo MUST have: ```markdown # Project Name > One-line description ![Demo GIF or Screenshot](./demo.gif) ## ✨ Features - Feature 1 - Feature 2 ## 🚀 Quick Start ```bash git clone ... cd ... npm install # or pip install or docker compose up npm run dev ``` ## 🏗️ Architecture [Include a diagram — Mermaid, ASCII, or image] ## 📝 License MIT ``` --- ## 6. Blogging & Content Strategy ### Platform Strategy ``` Primary: Hashnode (custom domain → blog.joel.dev) ├── Full posts live here ├── SEO optimized └── Custom domain = professional Secondary: Dev.to (syndication) ├── Cross-post from Hashnode ├── Larger community reach └── Use canonical URL pointing to Hashnode ``` ### Content Calendar Template | Week | Topic | Format | Platform | |------|-------|--------|----------| | W1 | "Building an LLM Router That Saves 80%" | Deep-dive tutorial | Hashnode → Dev.to | | W2 | "My ThinkPad Portfolio: Astro + xterm.js" | Project showcase | Hashnode → Dev.to | | W3 | "RAG from Scratch with LlamaIndex" | Tutorial series (Part 1) | Hashnode | | W4 | "How I Published in IEEE as an Undergrad" | Story/case study | LinkedIn → Hashnode | ### Post Frequency - **Minimum:** 2 posts/month - **Ideal:** 1 post/week - **Quality over quantity** — One excellent deep-dive > four shallow posts ### Hashnode Setup ``` 1. Create account at hashnode.com 2. Create a blog 3. Go to Dashboard → Domain 4. Map custom domain: blog.joel.dev 5. Add CNAME record: blog.joel.dev → hashnode.network 6. Enable RSS feed (auto-enabled) 7. Set canonical URL pattern for syndication ``` --- ## 7. Hackathon Strategy ### Target Hackathons (Priority Order) | Hackathon | Why | When | Prize/Benefit | |-----------|-----|------|--------------| | **Smart India Hackathon (SIH)** | Government-backed, CV gold | Aug-Dec | ₹1L + recognition | | **Flipkart GRID** | Direct hiring pipeline | Jul-Sep | Internship/FTE offers | | **Goldman Sachs Hackathon** | Marquee company direct access | Oct-Nov | Interview fast-track | | **Google Solution Challenge** | Global prestige | Jan-Mar | Google mentorship | | **MLH Hackathons** | Portfolio building | Monthly | Varied | | **HackWithInfy (Infosys)** | Power Programmer offer | Jun-Aug | ₹9+ LPA direct offer | ### Hackathon Prep Kit ``` Before every hackathon, prepare: ☐ Boilerplate repos ready to clone: - FastAPI + React template - Streamlit ML template - Docker Compose starter ☐ Common APIs pre-configured: - LiteLLM proxy for LLM access - HuggingFace Inference API key - Google Cloud credits ☐ Presentation template: - Problem slide - Solution slide - Demo slide - Tech stack slide - Team slide - Future scope slide ☐ Team role assignment: - Who does frontend? - Who does backend/ML? - Who does presentation? - Who does deployment? ``` ### Hackathon Execution Framework ``` Hour 0-1: Problem understanding + solution brainstorm Hour 1-2: Architecture + task breakdown Hour 2-8: Build MVP (parallel work) Hour 8-10: Integration + testing Hour 10-12: Polish + presentation + demo recording ``` --- ## 8. Cold Email Templates ### Rules for Cold Emails 1. **< 120 words** — Busy people won't read essays 2. **Signal-based** — Reference their specific work 3. **Clear ask** — "15-minute call" not "any opportunity" 4. **No attachments** — Link to your portfolio instead 5. **Follow up once** — After 5-7 days, then stop ### Template 1: Startup Founder (Internship) ``` Subject: Your RAG pipeline talk at PyData → internship interest Hi [Name], I watched your talk on [specific topic] at [event] and was impressed by [specific insight]. I'm building something similar — [your project, 1 sentence]. I'm a CS student at VIT Chennai with a published IEEE paper on federated learning and experience with [relevant tech]. I'd love to contribute to [company name] this summer. Portfolio: [link] GitHub: [link] Would you have 15 minutes for a call this week? Best, Joel Thomas ``` ### Template 2: Engineering Manager (Referral) ``` Subject: [Mutual connection] suggested I reach out — [Role] at [Company] Hi [Name], [Mutual connection] mentioned you're hiring for [role]. I'm an AI/ML engineer with published research in federated learning and hands-on experience building LLM routing systems. What caught my attention about [Company] is [specific recent work — blog post, launch, paper]. My portfolio: [link] (it's a ThinkPad terminal — try typing "help") Could we do a quick 15-min call? Joel Thomas VIT Chennai '27 | IEEE Published | 4x Certified ``` ### Template 3: Recruiter (Direct Application) ``` Subject: AI/ML Engineer — published researcher, 4 certifications Hi [Name], I'm a final-year CS student at VIT Chennai applying for [specific role]. Two things that set me apart: 1. Published IEEE researcher (federated learning) 2. Built an LLM routing system saving 80% on API costs Portfolio: [link] | GitHub: [link] Happy to share more context over a quick call. Joel Thomas ``` --- ## 9. Content Formats That Work ### Format 1: Deep-Dive Tutorial ```markdown Title: "Building an LLM Router from Scratch — 80% Cost Savings" Structure: 1. The problem (why routing matters) 2. Architecture overview (with diagram) 3. Implementation (step-by-step with code) 4. Results (before/after cost comparison) 5. Lessons learned 6. Code repo link Length: 2000-3000 words Time to write: 4-6 hours Expected reach: 500-2000 views ``` ### Format 2: "I Fixed It" Post ```markdown Title: "How I Fixed xterm.js Crashing in Astro SSR (Dynamic Imports)" Structure: 1. The error (exact error message + screenshot) 2. What I tried (failed attempts — builds trust) 3. The root cause (why it happens) 4. The fix (working code) 5. Prevention (how to avoid in future) Length: 800-1200 words Time to write: 1-2 hours Expected reach: 200-500 views (but high SEO value!) ``` ### Format 3: Case Study ```markdown Title: "How I Published My First IEEE Paper as a 2nd-Year Undergrad" Structure: 1. Context (who I am, why I started) 2. The research journey (timeline, challenges) 3. Key decisions (topic selection, methodology) 4. Writing the paper (tools, process, reviews) 5. Submission and acceptance 6. Impact on career 7. Advice for other students Length: 1500-2500 words Time to write: 3-4 hours Expected reach: 1000-5000 views (high shareability) ``` ### Format 4: Comparison/Analysis ```markdown Title: "Astro vs Next.js vs Vite for Developer Portfolios (2026)" Structure: 1. Criteria (performance, DX, flexibility, SEO) 2. Comparison table 3. Deep dive on each 4. My recommendation (with reasoning) 5. When to choose each Length: 1500-2000 words Time to write: 3-5 hours Expected reach: 300-1000 views ``` --- ## 10. Indian AI Market Landscape (2026) ### Funding Snapshot (Q1 2026) | Metric | Value | |--------|-------| | Total AI funding (India, Q1 2026) | $1.48 billion | | Largest round | Sarvam AI ($1.5B valuation) | | Number of funded AI startups | 200+ | | Average seed round | $2-5M | | Average Series A | $10-30M | ### Hot AI Startups Hiring | Company | Valuation | Focus | Hiring For | |---------|-----------|-------|------------| | **Sarvam AI** | $1.5B | Indian language LLMs | ML Engineers, Full-Stack | | **Krutrim** | $1B+ | AI infrastructure | Systems Engineers | | **Observe.AI** | $500M+ | Contact center AI | ML Engineers, NLP | | **Hasura** | $500M+ | GraphQL + AI | Full-Stack, DevRel | | **Postman** | $5.6B | API + AI | Full-Stack, ML | | **Razorpay** | $7.5B | Fintech + AI | Backend, ML | ### In-Demand Skills (India, 2026) ``` Tier 1 (Highest demand, highest pay): ├── LLM fine-tuning (LoRA, QLoRA) ├── RAG systems ├── ML infrastructure (MLOps) └── AI agent architectures Tier 2 (High demand): ├── Full-stack + AI integration ├── Cloud architecture (AWS/GCP) ├── Data engineering └── Computer vision Tier 3 (Stable demand): ├── Traditional ML (sklearn, XGBoost) ├── Frontend (React, Next.js) ├── Backend (Node.js, Django) └── Mobile (React Native, Flutter) ``` ### Salary Benchmarks (AI/ML, India, 2026) | Experience | Range (LPA) | Companies | |-----------|-------------|-----------| | Intern | ₹3-8 LPA | Startups, service companies | | Fresh grad (Dream) | ₹6-10 LPA | Product companies | | Fresh grad (Super Dream) | ₹10-20 LPA | Top product companies | | Fresh grad (Marquee) | ₹20-50 LPA | FAANG, quant firms | | 1-3 years | ₹15-40 LPA | Product companies | | 3-5 years | ₹30-70 LPA | Senior roles, startups | --- ## 11. Certification ROI Analysis ### ROI Comparison Table | Certification | Cost | Study Time | Salary Impact | ROI Score | |--------------|------|-----------|---------------|-----------| | **AWS Cloud Practitioner** | ₹8,500 ($100) | 2 weeks | +₹1-2 LPA | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **AWS Solutions Architect Associate** | ₹12,750 ($150) | 4-6 weeks | +₹2-4 LPA | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Google Cloud Associate CE** | ₹17,000 ($200) | 4 weeks | +₹1-3 LPA | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Kubernetes (CKA)** | ₹31,500 ($375) | 6-8 weeks | +₹2-4 LPA | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **TensorFlow Developer** | ₹8,500 ($100) | 4 weeks | +₹1-2 LPA | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Meta Frontend Developer** | ₹3,500 (Coursera) | 6-8 weeks | +₹0.5-1 LPA | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Azure Fundamentals** | ₹5,400 ($65) | 1 week | +₹0.5-1 LPA | ⭐⭐⭐ | ### Priority Order for Joel ``` 1. ✅ AWS Cloud Practitioner (immediate — 2 weeks, cheap, high ROI) 2. ⬜ AWS Solutions Architect Associate (after Cloud Practitioner) 3. ⬜ TensorFlow Developer Certificate (complements ML research) 4. ⬜ Kubernetes CKA (for DevOps/MLOps roles) ``` ### Study Tips - **Use free resources first:** AWS Skill Builder, Google Cloud Skills Boost - **Practice exams are key:** Buy TutorialsDojo or Whizlabs practice tests (₹500-800) - **Hands-on > reading:** Use free tiers to build real things - **Schedule the exam first:** Having a deadline forces studying - **Student discounts:** Many providers offer 50% off for students (AWS Educate, etc.) --- ## 12. Files to Maintain Keep these files updated regularly as your "career operating system": ### `docs/personal/database.md` **Purpose:** Master record of all career assets. ```markdown # Career Database ## Education - VIT Chennai, B.Tech CSE, 2023-2027, CGPA: X.XX ## Certifications | Cert | Provider | Date | Credential ID | |------|----------|------|---------------| ## Publications | Title | Venue | Date | DOI | |-------|-------|------|-----| ## Projects | Name | Tech Stack | Status | Link | |------|-----------|--------|------| ## Skills Matrix | Skill | Level (1-5) | Last Used | |-------|------------|-----------| ``` ### `docs/personal/career_analytics.md` **Purpose:** Track applications, interviews, and outcomes. ```markdown # Career Analytics ## Applications Tracker | Date | Company | Role | Status | Notes | |------|---------|------|--------|-------| ## Interview Log | Date | Company | Round | Outcome | Learnings | |------|---------|-------|---------|-----------| ## Metrics - Applications sent: X - Response rate: X% - Interview conversion: X% - Offers received: X ``` ### `docs/personal/market_insights.md` **Purpose:** Track industry trends, salary data, and opportunities. ### `docs/personal/marketing_strategy.md` **Purpose:** Content calendar, branding goals, and campaign tracking. ### `docs/personal/self_improvement_plan.md` **Purpose:** Learning goals, skill gaps, and weekly/monthly objectives. ### `docs/personal/llm_context.md` **Purpose:** A document specifically formatted for LLM agents — contains your complete context (profile, goals, constraints) so any agent can understand your situation quickly. ```markdown # LLM Context — Joel Thomas ## Quick Context (for system prompts) I'm Joel Thomas, a CS student at VIT Chennai (graduating 2027). I'm targeting Marquee/Super Dream placements (≥₹10 LPA). I have published IEEE research, 4 certifications, and I'm building an AI-focused portfolio. ## Current Goals 1. Complete AWS Cloud Practitioner by end of July 2026 2. Build and publish RAG project by August 2026 3. Land a summer internship 4. Reach 200 LeetCode problems by October 2026 ## Constraints - Budget: Student budget (~₹5K/month for career expenses) - Time: Full-time student, ~4 hours/day available for career work - Location: Chennai, India (open to remote internships) ``` --- ## 13. Monthly Execution Checklist ### Every Week ``` ☐ Post 1x on LinkedIn (technical insight or project update) ☐ Solve 5+ LeetCode problems ☐ 1 hour of cert study ☐ Update any active project ☐ Engage with 5 LinkedIn posts in your niche ``` ### Every Month ``` ☐ Publish 1 blog post on Hashnode ☐ Cross-post to Dev.to ☐ Update GitHub profile README if needed ☐ Review and update career_analytics.md ☐ Apply to 2-3 internships or opportunities ☐ Attend 1 tech meetup or virtual event ☐ Review market_insights.md for new trends ``` ### Every Quarter ``` ☐ Audit LinkedIn profile (headline, about, featured) ☐ Update resume and portfolio ☐ Reassess gap analysis — what's closed, what's new? ☐ Set next quarter's goals in self_improvement_plan.md ☐ Reach out to 3 new people (cold email or LinkedIn) ☐ Review certification roadmap — next cert? ``` --- ## 14. Quick Reference ### Key Numbers | Metric | Target | |--------|--------| | LeetCode problems | 200+ by October 2026 | | Blog posts | 2/month minimum | | LinkedIn posts | 2/week | | GitHub contributions | Green every week | | Certifications | 5+ by placement season | | Projects (portfolio-worthy) | 4-6 | | Cold emails/month | 5-10 | ### Elevator Pitch (30 seconds) > "I'm Joel Thomas, a CS student at VIT Chennai building AI systems. I've published research on federated learning in IEEE, earned 4 industry certifications, and I'm currently building an intelligent LLM routing system that cuts API costs by 80%. I'm looking for AI/ML roles where I can apply research to production systems." ### Links to Maintain | Platform | URL | Update Frequency | |----------|-----|-----------------| | Portfolio | joel.dev | Monthly | | LinkedIn | linkedin.com/in/joelthomas | Weekly | | GitHub | github.com/joelthomas | Daily (commits) | | Blog | blog.joel.dev | 2x/month | | LeetCode | leetcode.com/joelthomas | 5x/week | --- *Last updated: June 2026 | Author: Joel Thomas*

args.CodeContent: # Writing Agent Handoff Documents > A comprehensive guide for AI agents and developers on creating effective handoff documents that enable seamless continuation of work across agent sessions, conversations, and team members. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [Why Handoffs Matter](#why-handoffs-matter) 2. [The 3-Place Rule](#three-place-rule) 3. [Document Structure — Complete Reference](#document-structure) 4. [Complete Handoff Template](#complete-template) 5. [Writing Each Section](#writing-each-section) 6. [File Paths & Inventories](#file-inventories) 7. [Subagent Prompt Documentation](#subagent-prompts) 8. [Inline Config Schemas](#inline-configs) 9. [Port Assignments & Service Maps](#port-assignments) 10. [Credential Placeholders](#credential-placeholders) 11. [Error Tracking](#error-tracking) 12. [Conversation ID & Transcript Access](#conversation-ids) 13. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 14. [Pre-Handoff Verification Checklist](#verification-checklist) 15. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. Why Handoffs Matter AI agents have **limited context windows** and **no persistent memory** between sessions. Without a handoff document: - The next agent starts from scratch, re-reading the entire codebase - Design decisions are lost — the next agent might contradict them - Partially completed work is abandoned or duplicated - Time is wasted re-discovering project state A good handoff document is the difference between a 5-minute continuation and a 2-hour re-onboarding. ### Who Reads Handoffs? 1. **Future AI agents** — May be the same model or a different one. They have limited context, so conciseness matters. 2. **Human developers** — May need to understand what was built, why, and what's left. 3. **You (the writing agent)** — If resumed in the same conversation, you may need to re-read your own handoff. ### The Core Principle > **Write the document you wish you had received.** If you were a new agent dropped into this project with zero context, what would you need to know to continue effectively? --- ## 2. The 3-Place Rule **Always save the handoff in 3 locations:** | Location | Path | Why | |----------|------|-----| | **1. Artifact directory** | `/brain//AGENT_HANDOFF.md` | Accessible to the platform; appears in conversation artifacts | | **2. Project root** | `/AGENT_HANDOFF.md` | Accessible to any agent working on the project | | **3. Related projects** | Other project roots that interact with this one | Cross-project awareness | ### Why Redundancy? - **Artifact dir** might not be visible to the next agent if they're in a different conversation - **Project root** might not be found if the agent is dropped into a different workspace - **Related projects** prevent "island" effect where one project doesn't know about changes in another ### Example If you're working on a portfolio project that connects to an AI workstation: ``` d:\portfolio-draft\AGENT_HANDOFF.md # Project root d:\agentos\AGENT_HANDOFF.md # Related project C:\Users\joel\.gemini\...\AGENT_HANDOFF.md # Artifact directory ``` --- ## 3. Document Structure — Complete Reference Every handoff should contain these sections in this order: ``` 1. User Profile — Who the user is and their preferences 2. System Specs — Hardware, OS, environment 3. Project Status — Overall progress (one paragraph) 4. File Inventory — Every file created/modified, with paths and sizes 5. What's Done — Completed work, with verification notes 6. What's Pending — Remaining tasks, in priority order 7. How to Continue — Step-by-step for the next agent 8. Architecture Reference — System design, component relationships 9. Port Assignments — Service → port mapping 10. Credentials Needed — What API keys/tokens are required (names only!) 11. Open Questions — Unresolved decisions 12. Design Decisions — Decisions made and their rationale 13. Error Log — What went wrong and why 14. Conversation ID — For transcript access ``` > **⚠️ Keep the total document under 15 KB.** The next agent's context window is limited. A 50 KB handoff wastes context space. If you need more detail, link to separate files. --- ## 4. Complete Handoff Template Copy this template and fill in the sections: ```markdown # Agent Handoff — [Project Name] **Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM TZ] **Agent:** [Model name/version] **Conversation ID:** [UUID] **Status:** [In Progress / Completed / Blocked] --- ## User Profile - **Name:** [Full name] - **Role:** [Student/Developer/etc.] - **Location:** [City, Country, Timezone] - **Institution:** [If applicable] - **Communication style:** [Concise/detailed, preferences] - **Key context:** [Anything the next agent should know about the user] ## System Specs | Spec | Value | |------|-------| | OS | [e.g., Windows 11 23H2] | | Shell | [e.g., PowerShell 7.x] | | WSL | [e.g., Ubuntu 24.04 (agentos)] | | GPU | [e.g., NVIDIA RTX 4060, CUDA 12.x] | | Node.js | [e.g., v22.x] | | Python | [e.g., 3.12.x] | | RAM | [e.g., 16GB] | | IDE | [e.g., VS Code 1.9x] | ## Project Status [One paragraph summary: What is this project? What's the current state? What percentage is done?] ## File Inventory | File | Size | Status | Notes | |------|------|--------|-------| | `d:\project\src\index.ts` | 2.4 KB | ✅ Created | Entry point | | `d:\project\src\router.ts` | 5.1 KB | ✅ Created | LLM routing logic | | `d:\project\config.yaml` | 1.2 KB | ⚠️ Partial | Missing cache config | | `d:\project\tests\router.test.ts` | — | ❌ Not started | Unit tests needed | ## What's Done - [x] [Task 1] — [Brief description of what was done and how to verify] - [x] [Task 2] — [Brief description] - [x] [Task 3] — [Brief description] ### Verification ```bash # How to verify the work is correct npm run dev # Should start on port 4321 npm run test # Should pass X/Y tests curl localhost:4000/health # Should return 200 ``` ## What's Pending Priority order (most important first): 1. **[Task]** — [What needs to be done, why, any constraints] 2. **[Task]** — [What needs to be done] 3. **[Task]** — [What needs to be done] ## How to Continue Step-by-step instructions for the next agent: 1. Read the file inventory above to understand the project layout 2. [Step 2 — specific action] 3. [Step 3 — specific action] 4. Run `[verification command]` to check your work 5. Update this handoff document when done ## Architecture Quick Reference ``` [ASCII diagram or Mermaid of the system architecture] Component A (port XXXX) ↓ Component B (port YYYY) ↓ Component C (port ZZZZ) ``` ## Port Assignments | Port | Service | Status | |------|---------|--------| | 4000 | LiteLLM Proxy | ✅ Running | | 4321 | Astro Dev Server | ⚠️ Not started | | 5678 | n8n | ✅ Running | | 11434 | Ollama | ✅ Running | ## Credentials Needed | Name | Where to Set | Purpose | |------|-------------|---------| | `OPENAI_API_KEY` | `.env` | LLM API access | | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | `.env` | Claude access | | `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` | `.env` | Bot notifications | > ⚠️ NEVER include actual credential values. Only names and where to set them. ## Open Questions - [ ] Should we use SQLite or PostgreSQL for routing logs? - [ ] Is the ThinkPad animation too slow on mobile? - [ ] Should easter eggs be documented or left for discovery? ## Design Decisions | Decision | Choice | Rationale | |----------|--------|-----------| | CSS framework | Vanilla CSS | Maximum control for 3D transforms | | Terminal library | xterm.js | Best terminal emulation, active maintenance | | Hosting | Vercel | Free tier, Astro adapter available | | Font | IBM Plex Mono | Matches ThinkPad brand aesthetic | ## Error Log | Time | Agent/Task | Error | Resolution | |------|-----------|-------|------------| | 14:30 | Subagent #1 | npm install timeout | Re-ran with `--legacy-peer-deps` | | 15:45 | Main agent | xterm SSR crash | Switched to `client:only="react"` | ## Conversation ID ``` Conversation: [UUID] Transcript: \brain\[UUID]\.system_generated\logs\transcript.jsonl ``` To access the full conversation history: ```bash # View the last 20 steps tail -n 20 "\brain\[UUID]\.system_generated\logs\transcript.jsonl" # Search for specific actions grep "write_to_file" "\brain\[UUID]\.system_generated\logs\transcript.jsonl" ``` ``` --- ## 5. Writing Each Section ### User Profile Keep it brief but include information that affects agent behavior: ```markdown ## User Profile - **Name:** Joel Thomas - **Role:** CS student, VIT Chennai (2027) - **Timezone:** IST (UTC+5:30) - **Communication:** Prefers concise responses, bullet points, code examples - **Key context:** Targeting Marquee placement (≥₹20 LPA). Has published IEEE research. Working on portfolio + AI workstation projects simultaneously. ``` **Why it matters:** An agent that knows the user is a student in India will make different technology recommendations than one that thinks the user is a senior engineer at Google. ### System Specs Include everything that affects build/run behavior: ```markdown ## System Specs | Spec | Value | |------|-------| | OS | Windows 11 Pro 23H2 (Build 22631) | | Shell | PowerShell 7.4.x | | WSL | Ubuntu 24.04 "agentos" (WSL2, systemd=true) | | GPU | NVIDIA RTX 4060 8GB (Driver 555.99, CUDA 12.4) | | Node.js | v22.12.0 (via nvm) | | Python | 3.12.3 (system), 3.12.7 (venv) | | Package manager | pnpm 9.x (preferred over npm) | | RAM | 16GB DDR5 | | Storage | 1TB NVMe (D: drive for projects) | ``` **Why it matters:** Knowing the OS is Windows + WSL prevents the next agent from writing Linux-only instructions. Knowing there's a GPU affects ML model choices. ### Project Status One paragraph. Be honest about what works and what doesn't: ```markdown ## Project Status The portfolio site is ~70% complete. The Astro project structure, ThinkPad CSS shell, and basic terminal (xterm.js) are working. Commands (help, about, skills) work. What's missing: CRT overlay effects, mobile responsiveness, easter egg commands, deploy config, and the contact form. The dev server runs without errors on port 4321. ``` --- ## 6. File Paths & Inventories ### Always Use Absolute Paths ```markdown ## File Inventory | File | Size | Status | |------|------|--------| | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\pages\index.astro` | 1.2 KB | ✅ Complete | | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\components\Terminal.tsx` | 4.8 KB | ✅ Complete | | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\components\LaptopShell.astro` | 2.1 KB | ⚠️ Needs CRT overlay | | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\styles\global.css` | 3.2 KB | ✅ Complete | | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\styles\laptop.css` | 5.7 KB | ✅ Complete | | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\styles\crt.css` | 0 KB | ❌ Not created | | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\lib\commands.ts` | 3.4 KB | ✅ Complete | | `d:\portfolio-draft\src\lib\easter.ts` | 0 KB | ❌ Not created | ``` ### Why Include File Sizes? File sizes help the next agent: 1. **Verify files exist** — A 0 KB file means it wasn't actually written 2. **Estimate context cost** — A 50 KB file is too large to read in full 3. **Detect partial writes** — A config file that should be 2 KB but is 200 bytes was likely truncated ### Verification Commands Always include how to verify the file inventory: ```bash # PowerShell: Verify all files exist and check sizes Get-ChildItem -Recurse d:\portfolio-draft\src\ | Select-Object FullName, Length | Format-Table -AutoSize # Bash (WSL): Same thing find /mnt/d/portfolio-draft/src -type f -exec ls -lh {} \; ``` --- ## 7. Subagent Prompt Documentation If you used subagents, **document their exact prompts** so they can be re-run: ```markdown ## Subagent Prompts ### Subagent 1: CSS Writer **Status:** ✅ Completed successfully **Conversation ID:** `abc-123-def` Prompt used: ``` Create the file `d:\portfolio-draft\src\styles\crt.css` with CRT monitor effects. Include: - Scanline overlay (repeating-linear-gradient) - Screen flicker animation (subtle opacity change) - Phosphor glow effect on text - VHS-style distortion (optional, triggered by class) All elements must have pointer-events: none to not block terminal interaction. Use CSS custom properties from global.css. ``` ### Subagent 2: Command System **Status:** ❌ Failed (timeout after 5 minutes) **Conversation ID:** `ghi-456-jkl` **Error:** Agent ran out of context before completing easter.ts Prompt used: ``` Create the easter egg command system in `d:\portfolio-draft\src\lib\easter.ts`. Include these hidden commands: matrix (rain animation), sudo (joke response), hack (fake hacking sequence), coffee (ASCII art), rm -rf (fake destruction). Export as easterEggCommands record matching the CommandHandler type from commands.ts. ``` **To re-run this subagent:** Use the same prompt above. The commands.ts file at `d:\portfolio-draft\src\lib\commands.ts` already exports the CommandContext type and CommandHandler type that easter.ts should use. ``` --- ## 8. Inline Config Schemas **Never just reference an external config file — include the schema inline.** ### Why? The next agent might not be able to read the referenced file (different workspace, file moved, etc.). Include enough of the schema that they can recreate or modify it. ### Bad (reference only) ```markdown ## Config See `d:\agentos\litellm\config.yaml` for LiteLLM configuration. ``` ### Good (inline schema) ```markdown ## LiteLLM Config Schema File: `d:\agentos\litellm\config.yaml` ```yaml model_list: - model_name: "simple" # Tier name (used in routing) litellm_params: model: "ollama/llama3.3:8b" # Provider/model format api_base: "http://localhost:11434" - model_name: "coding" litellm_params: model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514" api_key: "os.environ/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" # Reads from env router_settings: routing_strategy: "latency-based-routing" num_retries: 2 timeout: 60 litellm_settings: fallbacks: - coding: ["simple"] # If coding fails, use simple cache: true ``` **Key fields to modify:** - `model_list[].litellm_params.model` — Model identifier - `model_list[].litellm_params.api_base` — Only for local models - `litellm_settings.fallbacks` — Failover chain ``` --- ## 9. Port Assignments & Service Maps Always include a clear port table: ```markdown ## Port Assignments | Port | Service | Host | Status | Start Command | |------|---------|------|--------|---------------| | 4000 | LiteLLM Proxy | WSL (agentos) | ✅ systemd | `systemctl start litellm` | | 4321 | Astro Dev Server | Windows | ⚠️ Manual | `npm run dev` | | 5678 | n8n | WSL (agentos) | ✅ systemd | `systemctl start n8n` | | 9119 | Hermes Dashboard | WSL (agentos) | ⚠️ Manual | `hermes serve --dashboard` | | 11434 | Ollama | WSL (agentos) | ✅ systemd | `systemctl start ollama` | ``` Include the **start command** — the next agent shouldn't have to guess. --- ## 10. Credential Placeholders ### The Rule > **Include credential NAMES. Never include credential VALUES.** ### Pattern ```markdown ## Credentials Needed | Credential | Location | How to Obtain | |-----------|----------|---------------| | `OPENAI_API_KEY` | `~/.hermes/.env` | platform.openai.com → API Keys | | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | `~/.hermes/.env` | console.anthropic.com → API Keys | | `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` | `~/.hermes/.env` | Telegram → @BotFather → /newbot | | `GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` | n8n UI → Credentials | console.cloud.google.com → APIs & Services | | `GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET` | n8n UI → Credentials | Same as above | ### .env File Template ```bash # ~/.hermes/.env OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-proj-... # Replace with your key ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... # Replace with your key TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=7123456789:... # Replace with your token ``` ``` --- ## 11. Error Tracking Document errors so the next agent doesn't repeat mistakes: ```markdown ## Error Log ### Error 1: xterm.js SSR Crash - **When:** 14:30 IST - **Where:** `npm run build` step - **Error:** `ReferenceError: document is not defined` in Terminal.tsx - **Cause:** Static import of xterm.js runs during Astro SSR build - **Fix:** Changed to dynamic `import()` inside `useEffect()` + `client:only="react"` - **Prevention:** Never statically import browser-only libraries in Astro components ### Error 2: Subagent Timeout - **When:** 15:45 IST - **Agent:** Doc writer subagent (conv ID: `abc-123`) - **Error:** `failed to construct executor: no tool converter registered for moma_search` - **Cause:** Subagent type inherited incompatible tools - **Fix:** Used `self` subagent type instead, or wrote files directly - **Prevention:** Test subagent types with a simple task before delegating complex work ### Error 3: WSL Unicode Encoding - **When:** 16:00 IST - **Where:** PowerShell parsing of `wsl --list --verbose` output - **Error:** String comparisons failed due to null bytes in UTF-16LE output - **Fix:** Added `-replace '\x00', ''` cleanup step - **Prevention:** Always clean WSL CLI output before parsing in PowerShell ``` --- ## 12. Conversation ID & Transcript Access ### Why Include It? The conversation ID allows the next agent to: 1. **Read the full transcript** of what happened 2. **Understand context** that didn't make it into the handoff 3. **Find specific tool calls** or decisions 4. **Debug issues** by seeing exact error messages ### Format ```markdown ## Conversation Reference | Field | Value | |-------|-------| | Conversation ID | `de772d2a-7f19-4cdf-86c3-fc938572f220` | | Started | 2026-06-14T09:56:34Z | | Ended | 2026-06-14T13:10:05Z | | Model | Gemini 2.5 Pro | | Transcript | `C:\Users\joel\.gemini\antigravity\brain\de772d2a-...\logs\transcript.jsonl` | ### Useful Transcript Queries ```powershell # Find all files created Select-String "write_to_file" transcript.jsonl | Select-Object -First 20 # Find all errors Select-String '"status":"ERROR"' transcript.jsonl # Find user requests Select-String '"type":"USER_INPUT"' transcript.jsonl # Count tool calls (Select-String "tool_calls" transcript.jsonl).Count ``` ``` ### When to Reference the Transcript - **Always** include the conversation ID - **Optionally** include specific step numbers for critical decisions - **Never** rely on the transcript as the primary handoff — it's too large. The handoff document should be self-sufficient. --- ## 13. Gotchas & Lessons Learned ### 🚨 Gotcha #1: Keep Handoff Under 15 KB **Problem:** A 50 KB handoff document consumes ~12,500 tokens of the next agent's context. Combined with system prompt (~3,000 tokens) and tool definitions (~8,000 tokens), that leaves very little room for actual work. **15 KB budget (~3,750 tokens):** | Section | Budget | |---------|--------| | User Profile + System Specs | 500 bytes | | Project Status | 300 bytes | | File Inventory | 1,500 bytes | | What's Done + Pending | 2,000 bytes | | How to Continue | 1,500 bytes | | Architecture | 1,000 bytes | | Port/Creds/Errors | 2,000 bytes | | Config schemas (inline) | 3,000 bytes | | Open Questions + Decisions | 1,000 bytes | | Metadata (conv ID, etc.) | 500 bytes | | **Total** | **~13,300 bytes** | **What to cut if over budget:** 1. Remove verbose config examples — keep only the fields that need changing 2. Compress the file inventory — only list files that are relevant to remaining work 3. Remove the error log — unless errors are likely to recur 4. Remove completed tasks from "What's Done" — only keep recent ones 5. Link to external files instead of inlining large schemas ### 🚨 Gotcha #2: Always Verify File State Before Writing Handoff **Problem:** Agents sometimes report files as "created" when they actually errored. Or report partial work as complete. The handoff then contains lies. **Solution:** Before writing the handoff, verify every file you claim exists: ```bash # PowerShell: Check all claimed files exist $files = @( "d:\portfolio-draft\src\pages\index.astro", "d:\portfolio-draft\src\components\Terminal.tsx", "d:\portfolio-draft\src\styles\global.css" ) foreach ($f in $files) { if (Test-Path $f) { $size = (Get-Item $f).Length Write-Host "✅ $f ($size bytes)" } else { Write-Host "❌ MISSING: $f" } } ``` ```bash # Bash: Same verification for f in \ /mnt/d/portfolio-draft/src/pages/index.astro \ /mnt/d/portfolio-draft/src/components/Terminal.tsx \ /mnt/d/portfolio-draft/src/styles/global.css do if [ -f "$f" ]; then echo "✅ $f ($(wc -c < "$f") bytes)" else echo "❌ MISSING: $f" fi done ``` **Also verify the project builds:** ```bash cd d:\portfolio-draft && npm run build # If this fails, the handoff should note the build error ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #3: Agents May Have Written More or Less Than Reported **Problem:** When using multiple subagents, some may write files that weren't requested, and others may fail silently. The project state may differ from what any single agent "thinks" happened. **Solution:** Always check the actual directory state: ```bash # What files actually exist? (compare to your inventory) Get-ChildItem -Recurse d:\portfolio-draft\src\ -File | Select-Object FullName, Length, LastWriteTime | Sort-Object LastWriteTime -Descending | Format-Table -AutoSize ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #4: Don't Assume the Next Agent Knows Your Tools **Problem:** You might have used specific tools, commands, or APIs. The next agent might not have the same tools available. **Solution:** Include explicit commands, not tool references: ```markdown # Bad "Use the write_to_file tool to create config.yaml" # Good "Create `d:\project\config.yaml` with the following content: ..." ``` ### 🚨 Gotcha #5: Timestamps Are Critical **Problem:** Without timestamps, the next agent can't tell if the handoff is from 5 minutes ago or 5 days ago. Stale handoffs can be dangerous. **Solution:** Always include: - When the handoff was written - When key events happened - When services were last verified as running --- ## 14. Pre-Handoff Verification Checklist Run through this checklist before writing the final handoff: ```markdown ## Pre-Handoff Verification ### Files - [ ] All claimed files actually exist on disk - [ ] File sizes match expectations (no 0-byte files) - [ ] No extra files were created that aren't documented ### Build - [ ] Project builds without errors (`npm run build` or equivalent) - [ ] Dev server starts without errors (`npm run dev`) - [ ] No TypeScript errors (`npx tsc --noEmit`) ### Services - [ ] All services listed in port map are actually running (or correctly noted as stopped) - [ ] Service health checks pass ### Content - [ ] Handoff is under 15 KB - [ ] All file paths are absolute - [ ] No actual credentials are included - [ ] Config schemas are included inline (not just referenced) - [ ] Subagent prompts are documented for any failed subagents - [ ] Error log includes resolution or workaround for each error - [ ] Conversation ID is included ### Quality - [ ] A new agent could continue from this handoff without asking clarifying questions - [ ] Design decisions include rationale (not just "we chose X") - [ ] Open questions are actionable (not vague) ``` --- ## 15. Quick Reference ### Handoff Document Anatomy ``` AGENT_HANDOFF.md ├── Header (date, agent, conversation ID, status) ├── User Profile (who, preferences) ├── System Specs (OS, tools, versions) ├── Project Status (one paragraph) ├── File Inventory (path, size, status table) ├── What's Done (checkbox list with verification) ├── What's Pending (priority-ordered list) ├── How to Continue (step-by-step) ├── Architecture Reference (ASCII diagram) ├── Port Assignments (table) ├── Credentials Needed (names only!) ├── Open Questions (decision points) ├── Design Decisions (table with rationale) ├── Error Log (what went wrong, why, fix) └── Conversation ID (for transcript access) ``` ### Size Targets | Document | Target Size | Max Size | |----------|------------|----------| | Full handoff | 10-12 KB | 15 KB | | File inventory | 1-2 KB | 3 KB | | Inline configs | 1-3 KB | 5 KB | | Error log | 0.5-1 KB | 2 KB | ### Save Locations ``` 1. /brain//AGENT_HANDOFF.md 2. /AGENT_HANDOFF.md 3. /AGENT_HANDOFF.md ``` ### Verification Commands ```powershell # Check all files exist Get-ChildItem -Recurse \src\ -File | Select FullName, Length # Check project builds cd && npm run build # Check handoff size (Get-Item AGENT_HANDOFF.md).Length # Should be < 15360 bytes ``` --- *Last updated: June 2026 | Author: Joel Thomas*
